title: Suppression windows + admin override flow for SLO-triggered auto actions (with audit logging)
preview:
Context
Auto-rollback/pause is being wired to SLO burn-rate evaluation. We need a safe, auditable way to suppress auto actions during known incidents / telemetry issues / planned experiments, and an admin-only override path that is time-bound and visible in Console + logs.

Problem
Without suppression/override controls:
- Oncall cannot quickly stop repeated auto actions during a known incident (or during monitor/telemetry degradation), causing churn and noise.
- We risk either disabling the feature broadly (losing protection) or allowing unsafe rollbacks (SLA risk).
- Compliance and incident retros need an explicit audit trail for: who suppressed, why, for what scope, and when it expired.

Goals
1) Suppression windows
- Allow creating a suppression that prevents auto actions (rollback/pause/traffic-shift) for a specific scope.
- Scope must support at minimum: service/route + model + region + customer tier, matching the rollout scope semantics.
- Suppression must be time-bound (TTL) with server-side enforcement.
- Suppression should not disable evaluation/telemetry collection; it should only suppress the action step.

2) Admin override flow
- Add admin-only endpoints to create/clear suppressions (and optionally extend) with required justification.
- Enforce RBAC permission checks (see INT-8832).
- Emit audit events for create/update/delete/expire operations and for action attempts suppressed due to an active suppression.

3) Audit logging
- Follow ADR-0128 audit trail schema requirements: required fields, retention expectations, and privacy constraints.
- Audit must cover both:
  a) administrative changes (who set suppression/override)
  b) controller decisions (auto action suppressed + why)

Non-goals
- Complex policy templates UX in this ticket (Console policy editor work tracked separately).
- Permanent suppressions (must always be time-bound).
- Fully autonomous incident detection (suppression is a manual/admin tool).

Proposed semantics
- Suppression applies to auto actions only. Rollout controller continues to evaluate SLO status and emits metrics, but chooses DECISION=SUPPRESSED instead of executing an action.
- Suppression is evaluated before any action decision is executed (rollback/pause/shift). If suppression is active, we skip execution and record suppression reason.
- Default max TTL: 24h (guardrail). Extension requires admin call + new audit event.
- Suppression precedence:
  - If multiple suppressions match, choose the most specific scope match; in practice we can treat as “any match suppresses actions” and include the selected suppression_id in audit.
- Expiration:
  - Expired suppressions should be treated as inactive; we should not require a background job for correctness. Optional cleanup job for storage hygiene.

API sketch (control plane / policy service)
- POST /v1/rollouts/suppressions
  - body: rollout_id (optional), service, route, model_version (optional), region (optional), customer_tier (optional), action_types (optional; default ALL), ttl_seconds, justification
- PATCH /v1/rollouts/suppressions/{suppression_id}
  - allow extend ttl_seconds (bounded), update justification (append-only preferred; ok to allow set but audit must include old/new)
- DELETE /v1/rollouts/suppressions/{suppression_id}
  - requires justification
- GET /v1/rollouts/suppressions?rollout_id=…&service=… (for Console)

Notes
- Implementation can live in policy/config service (preferred) as it already stores rollout-related policy and has RBAC hooks.
- Rollout controller reads suppression state via existing policy fetch path (avoid adding another dependency in controller).

Audit events
Emit events per ADR-0128 (see links) for:
- suppression.created
- suppression.updated (ttl extension)
- suppression.deleted
- suppression.expired (optional if we have a sweeper; otherwise omit)
- rollout.auto_action_suppressed (controller decision)

Audit fields to include (minimum)
- event_type
- timestamp
- actor_type (user/service)
- actor_id (user id / service name)
- actor_email (if user; if available)
- scope (service, route, model_version, region, customer_tier, rollout_id)
- suppression_id
- ttl_seconds + expires_at
- justification
- policy_version / config_version (if applicable)
- correlation_id / request_id
- evaluated_monitors (ids) + metrics_snapshot_ref (for suppressed controller actions only)

Guardrails
- TTL bounds: min 5m, max 24h (configurable constant; start hardcoded)
- Rate-limit admin endpoints (prevent accidental paging storms due to repeated toggles)
- Require non-empty justification (min length 15 chars) for create/delete/extend
- Ensure suppression evaluation is fail-safe:
  - If suppression lookup fails (policy service unavailable), default to NOT suppressed (continue existing safe behavior) but emit metric/controller log “suppression_lookup_failed”. (We debated fail-closed; SRE prefers fail-open to preserve protection.)

Open questions
- Should suppression optionally apply only to rollback but not pause? Proposal: allow action_types filter (ALL by default). PM/SRE to confirm.
- Should we allow rollout_id-only suppressions? Proposal: yes (most common during incidents), but still include service/route for searchability.

Implementation tasks
- Add suppression model + persistence in policy/config service (or existing rollout policy store).
- RBAC wiring for new endpoints (align with INT-8832 permission name).
- Controller: read suppression state from policy result and short-circuit execution; emit decision outcome + audit event.
- Console API: expose suppression list for rollout detail view (depends on ENG-4831).
- Metrics: increment “rollback_suppressed”/“pause_suppressed” with suppression_reason label (bounded set).

Refs
- Project scope item: “do not rollback during known incident overrides.”
- ADR-0128: rollout audit trail schema for auto actions.
- PR-28511: admin override suppress auto rollback (initial implementation; this ticket formalizes suppression windows + audit requirements).
text:
Context
Auto-rollback/pause is being wired to SLO burn-rate evaluation. We need a safe, auditable way to suppress auto actions during known incidents / telemetry issues / planned experiments, and an admin-only override path that is time-bound and visible in Console + logs.

Problem
Without suppression/override controls:
- Oncall cannot quickly stop repeated auto actions during a known incident (or during monitor/telemetry degradation), causing churn and noise.
- We risk either disabling the feature broadly (losing protection) or allowing unsafe rollbacks (SLA risk).
- Compliance and incident retros need an explicit audit trail for: who suppressed, why, for what scope, and when it expired.

Goals
1) Suppression windows
- Allow creating a suppression that prevents auto actions (rollback/pause/traffic-shift) for a specific scope.
- Scope must support at minimum: service/route + model + region + customer tier, matching the rollout scope semantics.
- Suppression must be time-bound (TTL) with server-side enforcement.
- Suppression should not disable evaluation/telemetry collection; it should only suppress the action step.

2) Admin override flow
- Add admin-only endpoints to create/clear suppressions (and optionally extend) with required justification.
- Enforce RBAC permission checks (see INT-8832).
- Emit audit events for create/update/delete/expire operations and for action attempts suppressed due to an active suppression.

3) Audit logging
- Follow ADR-0128 audit trail schema requirements: required fields, retention expectations, and privacy constraints.
- Audit must cover both:
  a) administrative changes (who set suppression/override)
  b) controller decisions (auto action suppressed + why)

Non-goals
- Complex policy templates UX in this ticket (Console policy editor work tracked separately).
- Permanent suppressions (must always be time-bound).
- Fully autonomous incident detection (suppression is a manual/admin tool).

Proposed semantics
- Suppression applies to auto actions only. Rollout controller continues to evaluate SLO status and emits metrics, but chooses DECISION=SUPPRESSED instead of executing an action.
- Suppression is evaluated before any action decision is executed (rollback/pause/shift). If suppression is active, we skip execution and record suppression reason.
- Default max TTL: 24h (guardrail). Extension requires admin call + new audit event.
- Suppression precedence:
  - If multiple suppressions match, choose the most specific scope match; in practice we can treat as “any match suppresses actions” and include the selected suppression_id in audit.
- Expiration:
  - Expired suppressions should be treated as inactive; we should not require a background job for correctness. Optional cleanup job for storage hygiene.

API sketch (control plane / policy service)
- POST /v1/rollouts/suppressions
  - body: rollout_id (optional), service, route, model_version (optional), region (optional), customer_tier (optional), action_types (optional; default ALL), ttl_seconds, justification
- PATCH /v1/rollouts/suppressions/{suppression_id}
  - allow extend ttl_seconds (bounded), update justification (append-only preferred; ok to allow set but audit must include old/new)
- DELETE /v1/rollouts/suppressions/{suppression_id}
  - requires justification
- GET /v1/rollouts/suppressions?rollout_id=…&service=… (for Console)

Notes
- Implementation can live in policy/config service (preferred) as it already stores rollout-related policy and has RBAC hooks.
- Rollout controller reads suppression state via existing policy fetch path (avoid adding another dependency in controller).

Audit events
Emit events per ADR-0128 (see links) for:
- suppression.created
- suppression.updated (ttl extension)
- suppression.deleted
- suppression.expired (optional if we have a sweeper; otherwise omit)
- rollout.auto_action_suppressed (controller decision)

Audit fields to include (minimum)
- event_type
- timestamp
- actor_type (user/service)
- actor_id (user id / service name)
- actor_email (if user; if available)
- scope (service, route, model_version, region, customer_tier, rollout_id)
- suppression_id
- ttl_seconds + expires_at
- justification
- policy_version / config_version (if applicable)
- correlation_id / request_id
- evaluated_monitors (ids) + metrics_snapshot_ref (for suppressed controller actions only)

Guardrails
- TTL bounds: min 5m, max 24h (configurable constant; start hardcoded)
- Rate-limit admin endpoints (prevent accidental paging storms due to repeated toggles)
- Require non-empty justification (min length 15 chars) for create/delete/extend
- Ensure suppression evaluation is fail-safe:
  - If suppression lookup fails (policy service unavailable), default to NOT suppressed (continue existing safe behavior) but emit metric/controller log “suppression_lookup_failed”. (We debated fail-closed; SRE prefers fail-open to preserve protection.)

Open questions
- Should suppression optionally apply only to rollback but not pause? Proposal: allow action_types filter (ALL by default). PM/SRE to confirm.
- Should we allow rollout_id-only suppressions? Proposal: yes (most common during incidents), but still include service/route for searchability.

Implementation tasks
- Add suppression model + persistence in policy/config service (or existing rollout policy store).
- RBAC wiring for new endpoints (align with INT-8832 permission name).
- Controller: read suppression state from policy result and short-circuit execution; emit decision outcome + audit event.
- Console API: expose suppression list for rollout detail view (depends on ENG-4831).
- Metrics: increment “rollback_suppressed”/“pause_suppressed” with suppression_reason label (bounded set).

Refs
- Project scope item: “do not rollback during known incident overrides.”
- ADR-0128: rollout audit trail schema for auto actions.
- PR-28511: admin override suppress auto rollback (initial implementation; this ticket formalizes suppression windows + audit requirements).

title: Post auto-rollback events to Slack via incident-bot (dedupe + rate limit to avoid paging storms)
preview:
Context
Auto-rollback/pause decisions are now emitted as structured audit events and surfaced in Console. On-call needs a fast, low-noise notification path when an auto action occurs, without creating paging storms (e.g., flapping rollouts, rapid step retries, or multiple regions firing simultaneously).

Goal
Update incident-bot to post concise Slack notifications for auto actions (rollback/pause/suppress + missing/stale monitor) with:
- consistent formatting
- links to the relevant dashboards + Console rollout view
- dedupe + rate limiting per rollout_id/service to prevent alert floods
- clear ownership routing (service channel vs oncall channel) without paging by default

Non-goals
- PagerDuty integration for auto-rollback (we will revisit after we have stable noise characteristics).
- Full incident auto-creation in Slack (separate workstream).

Event sources
- Consume rollout auto-action audit events from the event stream (emitted by redwood backend).
- Primary event types to handle:
  1) rollout.auto_action.executed (action=ROLLBACK|PAUSE|SHIFT_TO_BASELINE)
  2) rollout.auto_action.suppressed (reason=ADMIN_OVERRIDE|KNOWN_INCIDENT|INSUFFICIENT_SAMPLE|DEBOUNCE|STALE_MONITOR)
  3) rollout.slo_monitor.missing_or_stale

Slack delivery requirements
1) Channels
- Default: #eng-oncall (notify) for executed actions (ROLLBACK/PAUSE/SHIFT_TO_BASELINE).
- Secondary routing to service owner channel if known (config map in incident-bot): e.g., #eng-platform-rollouts, #runtime-oncall, etc.
- Suppressed + missing/stale monitor: post to #eng-sre (notify) (not #eng-oncall) unless suppression reason is "KNOWN_INCIDENT" (then do nothing to avoid noise during active incident).

2) Message format (single message per event)
Include:
- Action + short reason (first line):
  - "AUTO-ROLLBACK executed" / "AUTO-PAUSE executed" / "AUTO-ROLLBACK suppressed" / "SLO monitor stale"
- Rollout scope: service/route, model_version, baseline_version (if present), region, customer_tier
- Policy context: policy_name + policy_version
- SLO snapshot summary (safe + compact):
  - which signals tripped (p95/p99 latency, 5xx)
  - burn-rate windows used (fast/slow) and whether both confirmed
  - sample size summary + staleness timestamp if relevant
- Links:
  - Console rollout URL (rollout_id)
  - Grafana dashboard deep link (rollout_id filter)
  - Monitor definition link (if available)
- Correlation: event_id + trace/correlation_id

Privacy / compliance
- Do not include customer identifiers (org name, request ids, API keys) in Slack messages.
- OK to include customer_tier and region.

Noise controls (must-have)
- Dedupe: if the same action for the same rollout_id + step_id is seen within 10 minutes, suppress duplicates.
- Rate limit: max 3 messages / 5 minutes per rollout_id; additional events should be aggregated into a single summary message "(N similar events suppressed; see Console)".
- Threading: use one Slack thread per rollout_id in #eng-oncall so follow-on events (e.g., repeated suppressions) attach to the initial executed action.
- Backoff on Slack API errors; do not retry indefinitely (avoid backlog storms).

Acceptance criteria
- In staging, synthetic SLO breach triggers a single Slack message within 60s.
- Repeated evaluation ticks during the same breach do not spam Slack (verified by dedupe/rate limit).
- Executed rollback posts to #eng-oncall and service channel (if configured).
- Suppression events do not post to #eng-oncall.
- Missing/stale monitor events post to #eng-sre with actionable next step ("monitor missing/stale; rollout will fail-open per policy") and correct links.
- Unit tests cover:
  - dedupe keying
  - per-rollout rate limiting
  - thread id caching
  - formatting for each event type

Implementation notes
- incident-bot should maintain a small in-memory + Redis cache (if available) keyed by rollout_id: {thread_ts, last_sent_at, dedupe_window_map}.
- Prefer deterministic dedupe key: sha256(rollout_id + step_id + action + policy_version + fast_window + slow_window).
- Ensure we tag messages with environment (staging/prod) and region to avoid confusion during game days.

References
- GitHub: incident-bot PR: sources/github/incident-bot/pr-477-post-auto-rollback-events-to-slack.json
- Audit events: sources/github/redwood/pr-28435-add-rollout-audit-events-to-event-stream.json
- Alerting + dashboard routing discussion: sources/slack/eng-oncall/1770311020.json, sources/slack/eng-sre/1770619940.json

title: Plan + execute game day for SLO-triggered auto-rollback (pause/rollback/suppress) and capture follow-ups
preview:
## Goal
Run an incident-style game day to validate end-to-end behavior for SLO-driven auto-mitigation during rollouts:
- Detect latency/error SLO breach scoped to rollout (route/model version/region/tier)
- Apply configured policy action (pause vs rollback vs suppress)
- Produce auditable decision trail + Console visibility
- Avoid alert noise/paging storms
- Validate staleness behavior and “known incident” suppression

This ticket owns: planning, execution, and capturing concrete follow-ups (owners + tracking).

## References
- Game day plan (Drive): sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/site-reliability-engineering/auto-rollback-game-day-plan.json
- Runbook draft (Confluence): sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/auto-rollback-on-slo-breach-runbook.json
- Postmortem motivating gaps: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/postmortems/2026-02-17-rollout-latency-regression-auto-rollback-gaps.json
- Metrics naming standard: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/rollouts-slo-metrics-naming-and-labeling.json

## Participants / roles
- Incident commander: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Observability lead: Aisha Bello
- Rollout controller: Jada Williams
- Console / audit trail: Felix Schneider
- Policy engine: Jordan Lee
- Release/QA support: Leila Farouk

## Preconditions (must be true before we run)
1) Feature flag enabled in staging for one internal service/route (canary rollout type).
2) SLO monitors exist for the scoped dimensions (at minimum: p95 latency + 5xx error rate).
3) Incident-bot Slack notifications enabled in non-paging mode (notify-only channel).
4) Dashboards accessible to oncall group.
5) Runbook draft is up-to-date with expected controller behavior.

## Test environment
- Env: staging
- Candidate service/route: redwood-api /v1/chat.completions (internal canary route)
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1
- Tiers: internal/self-serve
- Rollout type(s): canary traffic split + step-based rollout

## Scenarios executed
### Scenario A — Fast burn latency regression during canary
Inject tail latency increase for canary model version only (simulate runtime regression):
- Expected: controller pauses rollout at current step (MVP) OR rolls back if policy action=rollback
- Expected: audit event emitted with monitor ids + burn rate + window state + sample sizes
- Expected: Slack notification (notify-only) + Grafana dashboard shows “auto decision”

### Scenario B — 5xx burst (error rate SLO breach)
Inject elevated 5xx (synthetic) for canary target only:
- Expected: immediate pause/rollback based on policy template (enterprise defaults more aggressive)
- Expected: no oscillation (debounce/holdoff respected)

### Scenario C — Monitor staleness / missing data
Simulate telemetry lag / monitor query returning stale timestamp:
- Expected: controller does NOT rollback based on missing/stale signals
- Expected: decision outcome recorded as “suppressed: monitor_stale” or “no_action: monitor_missing”
- Expected: alert fires to notify channel for “monitor stale/missing for active rollout” (non-paging)

### Scenario D — Known incident suppression window
Mark an active incident suppression window (simulate ongoing infra incident):
- Expected: SLO breach detected but action suppressed with reason “known_incident_override”
- Expected: audit event includes suppression metadata + who/what set suppression (admin override)

### Scenario E — Rollback executor safety / idempotency under retries
Force a partial failure during rollback execution (e.g., transient control plane timeout) and retry:
- Expected: rollback converges safely; idempotent execution; no double-traffic-shift

## Execution notes (2026-02-25)
- 10:00–10:10 PT: kickoff, checklist confirmed, dashboards open.
- 10:10–10:25 PT: Scenario A (latency) executed in us-west-2.
- 10:25–10:40 PT: Scenario B (5xx) executed in us-west-2.
- 10:40–10:55 PT: Scenario C (stale) executed by disabling telemetry feed for scoped labels.
- 10:55–11:15 PT: Scenario D (suppression) executed; verified audit trail + Console view.
- 11:15–11:35 PT: Scenario E (idempotency) executed; forced retry path.

## Results summary
- ✅ Auto-pause triggered correctly for fast-burn latency and 5xx scenarios once min-sample threshold reached.
- ✅ Audit events were emitted and visible via Console API for the rollout id (filter + pagination worked).
- ✅ Slack notification posted once per decision (no storm) and linked to dashboard + rollout id.
- ⚠️ Monitor staleness handling behaved inconsistently between latency and error-rate monitors (see Findings #1).
- ⚠️ Debounce/holdoff prevented oscillation, but delayed rollback longer than expected in Scenario B under low QPS (see Findings #2).
- ⚠️ One UI panel state did not refresh automatically after rollback; required manual reload (see Findings #5).

## Findings / gaps
1) **Staleness classification mismatch (latency vs error monitors).**
   - Observed: latency burn-rate endpoint returned `stale=true` with timestamp > 6m old; controller suppressed as expected.
   - Observed: error-rate endpoint returned `unknown` state with no staleness bit; controller treated as “no breach” (fail-open) and continued rollout.
   - Risk: silent miss on stale error-rate signals.
   - Owner: Aisha / Jordan

2) **Min-sample gating interacts badly with fast-burn expectations at low QPS.**
   - For internal/self-serve routes with low baseline traffic in staging, reaching min sample took ~7–9 minutes.
   - Policy template expects 5m fast burn; in practice we triggered at ~9m.
   - Need: staging override, or allow min-sample to be expressed as both absolute and % of expected RPS.
   - Owner: Sean / Jordan

3) **Suppression audit trail missing “suppression scope resolution.”**
   - Audit event includes suppression reason + policy version, but not the computed scope (e.g., “service default suppression applied” vs “rollout override suppression applied”).
   - Makes incident review harder.
   - Owner: Felix

4) **Alert routing: monitor missing/stale should page only when in production and rollout step > X%.**
   - Current alert rule in staging is notify-only (OK), but production rule proposal would page on any staleness.
   - Recommendation: page only if rollout is active + traffic shifted >= 25% + monitor missing/stale persists > 10m.
   - Owner: Aisha

5) **Console: audit timeline refresh bug after action executes.**
   - Timeline did not show the latest event until page refresh; API had the event.
   - Likely UI subscription/cache invalidation issue.
   - Owner: Felix / Hannah

6) **Rollback executor retries were safe, but the “rate-limited shift” took longer than expected.**
   - Step-down strategy respected limits (good), but total time to revert to baseline at 50% canary was ~4m.
   - For enterprise tier we may need a faster “emergency revert” mode with separate guardrails.
   - Owner: Jada / Ethan

## Follow-ups (created / to be created)
- [ ] Update monitor adapter to normalize staleness/unknown states across SLO types; ensure controller treats unknown as suppress + alert (ties to ENG-4826).
- [ ] Revisit defaults for min-sample gating; add staging template and document expected behavior (ties to ENG-4861 + PM-618).
- [ ] Extend audit schema to include suppression scope resolution + computed scope fields (ties to ADR-0128 + ENG-4831/ENG-4833).
- [ ] Tune alert rules for stale/missing monitors to avoid paging storms (ties to ENG-4840).
- [ ] Fix Console audit timeline refresh (ties to ENG-4833).
- [ ] Evaluate rollback executor “emergency revert” mode proposal for high-tier incidents (ties to ENG-4829).

## Exit criteria (met)
- All scenarios executed at least once in staging.
- Findings documented with owners and linked to existing workstreams.
- Runbook updated with observed behavior + gaps (Rafael to PR Confluence edits).

- ✅ Auto-pause triggered correctly for fast-burn latency and 5xx scenarios once min-sample threshold reached.
- ✅ Audit events were emitted and visible via Console API for the rollout id (filter + pagination worked).
- ✅ Slack notification posted once per decision (no storm) and linked to dashboard + rollout id.
- ⚠️ Monitor staleness handling behaved inconsistently between latency and error-rate monitors (see Findings #1).
- ⚠️ Debounce/holdoff prevented oscillation, but delayed rollback longer than expected in Scenario B under low QPS (see Findings #2).
- ⚠️ One UI panel state did not refresh automatically after rollback; required manual reload (see Findings #5).

## Findings / gaps
1) **Staleness classification mismatch (latency vs error monitors).**
   - Observed: latency burn-rate endpoint returned `stale=true` with timestamp > 6m old; controller suppressed as expected.
   - Observed: error-rate endpoint returned `unknown` state with no staleness bit; controller treated as “no breach” (fail-open) and continued rollout.
   - Risk: silent miss on stale error-rate signals.
   - Owner: Aisha / Jordan

2) **Min-sample gating interacts badly with fast-burn expectations at low QPS.**
   - For internal/self-serve routes with low baseline traffic in staging, reaching min sample took ~7–9 minutes.
   - Policy template expects 5m fast burn; in practice we triggered at ~9m.
   - Need: staging override, or allow min-sample to be expressed as both absolute and % of expected RPS.
   - Owner: Sean / Jordan

3) **Suppression audit trail missing “suppression scope resolution.”**
   - Audit event includes suppression reason + policy version, but not the computed scope (e.g., “service default suppression applied” vs “rollout override suppression applied”).
   - Makes incident review harder.
   - Owner: Felix

4) **Alert routing: monitor missing/stale should page only when in production and rollout step > X%.**
   - Current alert rule in staging is notify-only (OK), but production rule proposal would page on any staleness.
   - Recommendation: page only if rollout is active + traffic shifted >= 25% + monitor missing/stale persists > 10m.
   - Owner: Aisha

5) **Console: audit timeline refresh bug after action executes.**
   - Timeline did not show the latest event until page refresh; API had the event.
   - Likely UI subscription/cache invalidation issue.
   - Owner: Felix / Hannah

6) **Rollback executor retries were safe, but the “rate-limited shift” took longer than expected.**
   - Step-down strategy respected limits (good), but total time to revert to baseline at 50% canary was ~4m.
   - For enterprise tier we may need a faster “emergency revert” mode with separate guardrails.
   - Owner: Jada / Ethan

## Follow-ups (created / to be created)
- [ ] Update monitor adapter to normalize staleness/unknown states across SLO types; ensure controller treats unknown as suppress + alert (ties to ENG-4826).
- [ ] Revisit defaults for min-sample gating; add staging template and document expected behavior (ties to ENG-4861 + PM-618).
- [ ] Extend audit schema to include suppression scope resolution + computed scope fields (ties to ADR-0128 + ENG-4831/ENG-4833).
- [ ] Tune alert rules for stale/missing monitors to avoid paging storms (ties to ENG-4840).
- [ ] Fix Console audit timeline refresh (ties to ENG-4833).
- [ ] Evaluate rollback executor “emergency revert” mode proposal for high-tier incidents (ties to ENG-4829).

## Exit criteria (met)
- All scenarios executed at least once in staging.
- Findings documented with owners and linked to existing workstreams.
- Runbook updated with observed behavior + gaps (Rafael to PR Confluence edits).

title: Perf-canary post-launch noise reduction (smoothing + baseline drift + region/SKU thresholds)
preview:
Context / problem
- After phase-1 rollout (us-east + limited model set) and enabling warning + paging alerts, we saw a spike in false positives. Primary drivers were:
  1) Baseline drift (slow change in fleet + scheduler behavior) causing “regression” deltas that were within normal variation.
  2) Region + hardware SKU variance: same model behaves differently across SKUs (and even within region depending on pool composition), so global thresholds were too tight for some deployments and too loose for others.
  3) Cardinality/schema churn: small label changes caused baseline lookups to miss (or compare apples-to-oranges) and resulted in spurious percent-change comparisons.
  4) Telemetry ingestion lag: late-arriving histograms produced misleading rolling-window stats.

Goal
Reduce false positives without materially reducing sensitivity to real regressions.

Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Building a full “quality eval + perf” combined signal (Applied ML owns).
- Customer traffic shadowing.

Proposed changes
A) Add smoothing / robust aggregation on the observed signal
- For each model+runtime_version+kernel_id+region+sku target, compute a smoothed p95 and tps estimate used for alerting.
  - Candidate: EWMA over 10–15m buckets (alpha tuned per metric) with guardrails for step changes.
  - Alternative: median-of-means across N buckets to downweight single-bucket spikes.
- Continue to display raw p95/p99 in dashboards for debugging; smoothing is alert-path only.

B) Baseline drift handling
- Baseline should be “sticky” for a given runtime_version+kernel_id+sku, but allow controlled drift updates when the system changes slowly.
- Add a baseline freshness + confidence score:
  - Inputs: sample_count, time_since_baseline_generated, variance observed during baseline build, and fraction of missing buckets due to ingest lag.
  - If confidence is low, degrade alert severity (page -> warn) and annotate payload.
- Define drift update policy:
  - Option 1 (recommended): rolling baseline with bounded change rate (e.g., max 1–2% per day) and only when no active regression.
  - Option 2: fixed baseline per release + manual recalibration (lower risk, more ops).
- Add explicit “baseline mismatch” detection:
  - If baseline key (model/version/runtime/sku/region) not found or schema mismatch detected, emit a distinct canary health alert (not a perf regression alert) and do NOT page.

C) Region/SKU-aware thresholds
- Introduce threshold overrides by {region, hardware_sku} with sane defaults:
  - Default thresholds remain per-model.
  - Overrides allow loosening/tightening based on measured variance.
- Keep config surface small to avoid operational overhead:
  - Allow wildcarding (e.g., region=* / sku=H100-80GB).
  - Hard cap the number of overrides per model to prevent config explosion.
- Provide guidance for calibration:
  - Use last 7 days of canary + baseline build data; choose threshold at ~2–3x typical bucket-to-bucket variance for p95, plus min sample constraints.

D) Anti-flap / dedupe improvements (coordinated with ENG-4878, but tracked here for post-launch polish)
- Confirm dedupe key includes: model_id, model_version, runtime_build, kernel_id (or kernel_family), region, sku, metric.
- Add “regression must persist for X of Y windows” logic (e.g., 3 of last 5) for paging only.
- Add a cooldown window after an alert resolves (e.g., 30m) to avoid bounce alerts during recovery.

E) Telemetry ingest lag guardrails
- Gate evaluation on data completeness:
  - If expected histogram buckets for the evaluation window are missing > N%, mark signal as “incomplete” and suppress perf regression alerts.
- Add a small dashboard panel for “perf-canary telemetry lag” next to perf panels to make this obvious in triage.

Implementation plan
1) Design review with Runtime + SRE + Telemetry
- Validate we are not hiding real regressions (esp. tail latency from kernel changes).
- Decide on smoothing method (EWMA vs median-of-means) and default params.

2) Baseline service changes
- Extend baseline artifact metadata to include: variance estimates, build timestamp, sample_count, and schema_hash.
- Implement baseline confidence score + mismatch detection.

3) Evaluator changes (perf-canary service)
- Add smoothed signals for p95 and tps.
- Add “incomplete data” gating based on telemetry lag.
- Update alert payload to include: smoothed vs raw values, baseline confidence, and whether a region/SKU override applied.

4) Config + tooling
- Extend control-plane config to accept region/SKU overrides with validation.
- Add a small calibration helper script (or notebook) that prints suggested thresholds from the last N days.

5) Rollout
- Enable changes first in us-east for top 3 models.
- Run in “shadow evaluation mode” for 48 hours (compute new decision but do not alert) and compare against current.
- If noise reduction meets targets, roll out to remaining models/regions.

Acceptance criteria / success metrics
- Paging false positives: <= 1 per 7 days across covered models in us-east during steady-state (excluding known rollout windows).
- Warning noise: <= 3 per day across covered models, with >80% having a clear attribution signal (queueing vs execution) and/or baseline confidence annotation.
- No missed known real regressions during validation (compare against staging replay and at least one controlled degradation test).
- Baseline mismatch events are routed as canary health warnings (non-page) with actionable next steps.

Notes / links
- Postmortem: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/postmortems/postmortem-perf-canary-false-positive-alert-storm.json
- Baseline drift discussion: sources/slack/eng-runtime/1768604412-baseline-drift.json
- Region/SKU thresholds discussion: sources/slack/eng-sre/1770468009-region-specific-thresholds.json
- Cardinality audit: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/observability/perf-canary-metrics-cardinality-audit.json
- Baseline week1 results: sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/serving-runtime/perf-canary-baseline-results-week1.json

Open questions
- Do we want separate baselines for Dedicated pools vs Hosted, even on the same SKU/region, given different scheduling pressure?
- For kernel attribution: is kernel_id stable enough, or should we bucket to kernel_family to avoid noisy baseline fragmentation?
- How should we treat fleet change events (new driver / CUDA / MIG config) as explicit baseline invalidation triggers vs slow drift?

Risks
- Over-smoothing can delay detection of fast regressions (kernel bug) by 10–20 minutes.
  - Mitigation: only smooth p95 for paging; keep a raw p99 “tripwire” warn-level alert with higher threshold.
- Region/SKU overrides can become unmaintainable.
  - Mitigation: caps + wildcarding + periodic pruning, and prefer variance-driven defaults.

Owners / stakeholders
- DRI: Nadia Rahman (baseline pipeline)
- Partner: Allison Grant (alerting standards), Sofia Ivanova (scheduler signals), Logan Wright (telemetry lag gating)
- On-call reviewer: Rafael Mendes

title: Private deployments: sync regional failover guidance with new trigger hierarchy + automation guardrails
preview:
## Context
We updated Hosted API + Dedicated regional failover automation (signal hierarchy, deterministic policy evaluation, rollback guardrails, dry-run/simulate tooling). Our Private deployment guidance still reflects the pre-upgrade world (manual runbooks + ad-hoc triggers), which is now out of sync and confusing for regulated / VPC / on-prem customers.

This ticket aligns Private docs with the new approach:
- Make it explicit what Redwood supports vs. what we recommend customers implement.
- Translate trigger hierarchy + anti-flap/rollback semantics into Private-friendly patterns (where customers may own routing + health evaluation).
- Ensure we are consistent with security boundaries (data residency, audit logs) and avoid implying unsupported automation.

## Goals
1) Reduce support load from ambiguous expectations (“Does Redwood automatically fail over my Private region?”).
2) Provide a clear reference architecture for Private multi-region failover using the same signal hierarchy concepts as Hosted/Dedicated.
3) Document supported integrations (Region Health Service API, audit event formats, recommended probes) and constraints (residency, KMS/HSM, customer-controlled routing).

## Non-goals
- Shipping new Private automation features in this ticket.
- Changing SLAs/contract language.
- Providing bespoke per-customer playbooks beyond the standard guidance doc.

## Primary doc(s) to update
- Confluence: sources/confluence/eng-private-deployments/day-2-ops/private-region-failover-guidance.json

## References / related specs
- ADR-042: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-042-failover-trigger-signal-hierarchy.json
- ADR-043: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-043-automated-routing-policy-rollback.json
- Security boundaries: sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/incident-response-security/failover-and-security-boundaries.json
- Region health service: sources/confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/region-health-service.json
- Hosted/Dedicated runbooks (for consistent terminology):
  - sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook.json
  - sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook.json

## What must be crystal clear in Private docs
### Supported (Redwood)
- Provide region health signals (where deployed) via Region Health Service API endpoints and/or exported metrics.
- Provide audit events for failover decisions/actions in Hosted/Dedicated; for Private, provide schema + recommended logging fields customers should record (who/what/why/evidence).
- Provide recommended signal hierarchy + default thresholds as guidance.
- Provide an operator procedure for controlled testing (staging first) and safe rollback patterns.

### Recommended (Customer)
- Customer-operated failover controller/policy evaluation (or equivalent) unless contract explicitly includes Redwood-managed routing.
- Corroboration-based trigger gating (reachability + burn-rate + capacity) with hold-down timers to avoid oscillation.
- Synthetic probes from each region (and from “client vantage points” if applicable) to distinguish internal vs external impairments.
- Explicit blast radius constraints (per-tenant/per-service) and rate limits on policy flips.

### Not supported / avoid implying
- Fully automatic cross-region failover “out of the box” for all Private deployments.
- Cross-region routing that violates data residency constraints.
- Using Redwood-controlled global anycast/DNS for customer-managed domains unless explicitly provisioned.

## Proposed content changes (outline)
1) Terminology + responsibility model
   - Define “control plane impairment” vs “regional data plane impairment” vs “partial degradation”.
   - RACI: customer-owned vs Redwood-owned components in Private.

2) Signal hierarchy (Private translation)
   - Authoritative: reachability (from multiple probe vantage points).
   - Next: 5xx burn / error budget burn.
   - Next: tail latency burn.
   - Next: capacity exhaustion / headroom gates.
   - Emphasize dampening + corroboration and missing-signal behavior (do not flip purely on missing telemetry).

3) Guardrails + rollback semantics
   - Anti-flap timers, minimum dwell time, max flips per hour.
   - Rollback triggers vs. “hold-down” semantics.
   - Auditability: record evidence signals, config version, operator approvals.

4) Data residency + security boundaries
   - Region pinning rules and when cross-region failover is disallowed.
   - KMS/HSM and secrets replication prerequisites.
   - Audit log retention expectations.

5) Controlled failover testing (“game day”) pattern
   - Pre-flight checklist (capacity headroom, probe readiness, comms, rollback owner).
   - Suggested success criteria (failover time, max error rate, p95 latency delta).
   - Post-test validation + follow-ups.

6) FAQ (must answer)
   - “Does Redwood automatically fail over Private?”
   - “How do I validate triggers without impacting customers?”
   - “What happens if the region health service is down?”
   - “How does this interact with Dedicated / Hosted if I use hybrid routing?”

## Acceptance criteria
- Updated Private failover guidance doc includes:
  - A “Supported vs Recommended vs Not Supported” section.
  - A table mapping each trigger type to: required corroboration, suggested windows, default dampening, and failure modes.
  - Explicit language on data residency constraints and examples.
  - A short runbook-style checklist for controlled failover tests.
- Reviewed by:
  - Simran Kaur (Private deployments), Miles Thornton (architecture), Victor Alvarez (network), Allison Grant (observability), and Security (via failover-and-security-boundaries doc owner).
- Support-ready: Dev Patel/Owen Phillips confirm the FAQ + phrasing matches how we want to answer customer tickets.

## Open questions
- For Private customers who run Redwood-managed routing inside a customer VPC: do we document optional integration with the failover controller (dry-run only) or keep it fully customer-owned?
- Do we want to provide a “reference implementation” snippet for policy evaluation config (non-executable) to reduce ambiguity?

## Implementation plan
- Draft doc updates in Confluence (Simran + Kira Thompson).
- Add a short “diff” note for Support/CS (what changed, what to tell customers).
- Link the updated doc from the operator-facing guide where relevant (docs PR: sources/github/redwood-docs/pr-982-regional-failover-operator-guide.json).

## Risk / pitfalls
- Over-promising automation: wording must avoid implying Redwood runs customer failover for Private.
- Security: accidental suggestion that customers can fail over across residency-restricted regions.
- Too much detail: keep thresholds as “starting points,” not commitments.

## Tracking
- Target: have updated guidance ready before expanding automation rollout beyond initial tiers/routes (see ENG-2424 quarterly cadence follow-up).

## Tracking
- Target: have updated guidance ready before expanding automation rollout beyond initial tiers/routes (see ENG-2424 quarterly cadence follow-up).

title: Define drift SLI and configure monitoring + alerts for Cost Model API
preview:
We need a single, auditable drift SLI definition for the Standard Cost Model initiative, plus a monitoring pipeline that continuously compares predicted (cost-model outputs) vs observed (infra spend / GPU-seconds-derived cost) and alerts when drift exceeds thresholds. This is required before Console flips cost breakdowns broadly and before Finance treats standard cost outputs as a reliable unit-econ primitive.

Context: We already saw a drift alert fire due to telemetry lag (see incidents thread 1769032199). This ticket formalizes drift semantics, adds lag-awareness + volume guardrails, and ships actionable dashboards + paging rules.

Deliverables:
- Drift SLI definition (v1) with explicit formulae, inclusion/exclusion rules, and windowing/lag policy.
- Scheduled drift monitoring job/pipeline emitting Prometheus metrics (bounded cardinality).
- Grafana dashboard + alert rules (warn/page) with runbook links and clear next steps.

Non-goals:
- Perfect cost reconciliation to invoices (billing system of record remains separate).
- Per-customer drift alerts at high cardinality (we will do top-K / aggregate-first in v1).

Primary stakeholders: Rafael Mendes (SRE), Logan Wright / Mei Lin (Telemetry), Ethan Park (API), Laura Bennett / Rishi Malhotra (FP&A).

title: Implement multi-window burn-rate alerts + paging severity routing for tiered SLOs
preview:
We need multi-window burn-rate alerting for tiered SLOs (Hosted vs Dedicated) and consistent paging severity mapping + on-call routing.

This ticket covers:
- Prometheus recording rules + alert rules for burn rate on primary SLIs (availability/success rate, latency, streaming reliability where applicable)
- Multi-window thresholds per ADR-0143 (fast-burn + slow-burn)
- Routing alerts to correct on-call rotations (SRE primary; add Infra/Fleet + Platform/Routing as secondary responders based on alert type)
- Severity mapping (SEV0/SEV1/SEV2) and what pages vs what goes to Slack-only
- Links to runbooks + consistent annotations (tier, region, endpoint_class)

Non-goals:
- Implementing the budget computation job (ENG-4934)
- Adding tier tags at ingest (ENG-4922)
- Building dashboards (separate ticket/PR in obs-pack)

title: Update API gateway timeouts/retries and circuit breakers so failover doesn’t amplify thundering herd
preview:
Context
During regional impairment + automated routing failover, our current gateway behavior can multiply load:
- clients retry (often aggressively) on timeouts/5xx
- gateway retries upstream on a subset of failures
- routing may redirect to another region while the original region is partially impaired

Net: thundering herd risk and cascading overload on the target region, plus extended tail latency while the system is trying to be “helpful”. This is a recurring pattern in incidents and showed up during rehearsals as elevated retries + timeouts right when failover begins.

Goal
Tune gateway retry/timeouts + circuit breaker behavior so the gateway fails fast and predictably during impairment, and so we shed load in a way that cooperates with routing/failover automation.

Non-goals
- Rewriting client SDK retry behavior (tracked separately in docs/SDK follow-ups)
- Changing customer SLA language
- Implementing routing policy logic in gateway (routing remains the source of truth)

Success criteria / acceptance tests
1) Retry amplification bounded
- For non-idempotent generation requests, gateway performs 0 automatic retries by default.
- For idempotent requests (explicit idempotency key) and safe endpoints, at most 1 retry on connect/reset/early upstream termination.
- Retry budget is enforced per request and cannot exceed configured wall-clock limit.

2) Fail-fast behavior during regional impairment
- Circuit breakers open quickly on sustained upstream timeouts/5xx to avoid queue buildup.
- When circuit is open or overload shed triggers, gateway returns a deterministic response with Retry-After.

3) Failover-friendly responses
- Responses during overload / impairment do not encourage immediate synchronized retries.
- Gateway does not “spin” between regions or repeatedly attempt the impaired upstream when routing is actively draining/redirecting traffic.

4) Observability
- Dashboardable metrics for: upstream timeouts, retry attempts, circuit state transitions, shed responses, Retry-After usage.
- Ability to correlate spikes to failover events (via annotations from controller / incident-bot).

Implementation plan (high level)
A) Retry policy changes
- Default: disable gateway retries for generation/chat POSTs unless request includes an idempotency key (Idempotency-Key header) AND failure mode is retry-safe.
- Retry-safe failures: connect timeout, TCP reset, upstream unavailable before request body fully sent.
- No retries on 4xx, no retries on 5xx once response headers started, no retries on explicit overload responses.
- Enforce per-request retry budget (max attempts + max time) and jittered backoff.

B) Timeout tuning
- Separate timeouts for:
  1) connect timeout
  2) upstream headers / first-token timeout (streaming)
  3) upstream idle timeout between chunks (streaming)
  4) total request timeout (non-streaming)
- Tighten defaults so we fail fast during brownouts and let routing take over rather than waiting long enough to stack work.

Proposed defaults (subject to staging validation)
- Connect timeout: 250–500ms (region-local) / 750ms (cross-region)
- Non-streaming upstream total timeout: 10s (was effectively higher for certain routes)
- Streaming:
  - time to first byte / first token: 2.5s default (tier-specific overrides allowed)
  - idle between chunks: 10s (prevents hanging sockets from tying up workers)

C) Circuit breakers / outlier detection
- Per-upstream-cluster circuit breaker keyed by (service, region, route class).
- Trip criteria uses a rolling window (short) and burn-rate style signal:
  - open when timeout rate OR 5xx rate crosses threshold AND corroborated by at least one additional symptom (e.g., upstream queue depth / connection failures) where available.
- Half-open with limited probe concurrency, jittered, and immediate re-open on failure.
- Outlier detection: eject consistently failing upstream hosts to reduce head-of-line blocking.

D) Load shedding + Retry-After
- When shed triggers (local overload or target region saturation), return 429/503 with Retry-After (and a stable error code) rather than letting clients hang until timeout.
- Standardize response semantics so SDKs can back off (ties into runtime + docs work).

E) Guardrails during failover
- If routing/control plane indicates region drain/redirect in progress (header/metadata from routing layer), gateway should avoid retrying the “old” region and should fail fast to allow the client to follow redirect or to allow routing to select a healthy region.
- Ensure we do not create redirect loops; track with a hop counter header (max 1 hop at gateway level).

Testing
- Staging:
  - synthetic impairment: inject upstream 5xx + latency, validate breaker opens and retry attempts remain bounded
  - load test: verify no worker pool exhaustion from hanging streams
  - regression: ensure normal p50/p95 unaffected for healthy region
- Production (game day support):
  - enable behind feature flag for a limited tenant set (internal + a few low-risk tenants)
  - monitor: retries, timeouts, shed rate, circuit transitions, customer error rate
  - rollback: flag off, restore previous timeout defaults

Rollout
1) Ship code behind flags (gateway.retry_policy_v2, gateway.streaming_timeouts_v2, gateway.circuit_breaker_v2)
2) Staging burn-in 48h
3) Prod canary (1% of Hosted API traffic, internal tenants)
4) Expand to 10% + Dedicated shared gateway paths where applicable
5) Make defaults for Hosted API; keep override for Dedicated per contract/tier

Links
- Related PRs:
  - https://sources/github/redwood/pr-19820-api-gateway-fallback-timeout-tuning.json
  - https://sources/github/redwood/pr-19827-add-graceful-degradation-retry-after.json
  - (client streaming reconnect companion) https://sources/github/redwood/pr-19821-streaming-reconnect-on-redirect.json
- Initiative overview: https://sources/linear/engineering/ENG-2411-regional-failover-automation-upgrade.json
- Burn-rate alerts: https://sources/linear/engineering/ENG-2418-add-failover-metrics-and-slo-burn-alerts.json

Open questions (resolved in comments)
- Whether to allow 1 retry for streaming starts without an idempotency key (decision: no; require explicit idempotency to avoid duplicate generations)
- How aggressive first-token timeout can be without hurting long-context workloads (decision: tiered override + route class; default tightened but not extreme)

title: Build and publish incident taxonomy dashboards (volume/MTTR/SLA by type + owner)
preview:
Goal
Build and publish the v1 incident taxonomy dashboards specified in Confluence, validate data accuracy against recent incidents, and add saved views per tier/team.

Spec / references
- Confluence spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/incident-taxonomy-dashboards-spec.json
- Taxonomy definitions: sources/confluence/eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/incident-taxonomy-v1-definitions.json
- Ownership + SLA matrix: sources/confluence/eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/incident-ownership-mapping-and-remediation-sla-matrix.json
- Cardinality guidance (follow-up): observability-pack PR notes referenced below

Deliverables
1) Dashboard: Incident overview (last 7d/30d)
- Incident count by incident_type (stacked by severity)
- Incident count by primary_owner (SRE/Runtime/Fleet/Routing/Observability/Control Plane/etc.)
- Top incident_type x owner table
- % Unknown/Unclassified over time

2) Dashboard: Speed of response
- MTTA / TTM (time-to-mitigate) and MTTR / TTF (time-to-fix) trends
- Breakdown by incident_type, severity, and tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
- Outlier table (p95) with incident links

3) Dashboard: SLA compliance
- Remediation SLA breach rate (breaches / total) by severity + tier
- Breaches by incident_type + owner
- Saved view(s) for Dedicated and Private that default to those tiers

4) Dashboard: Recurring incidents / drivers
- Top 10 incident types by count and by total minutes impacted
- “Repeat within 14 days” panel keyed by incident_type + affected service

Saved views (must exist)
- Hosted oncall view: default tier=Hosted, show last 7d, include Unknown panel
- Dedicated view: default tier=Dedicated, include SLA panels by default
- Private view: default tier=Private, include connectivity/config-related panels expanded
- Team views (filters pre-set): Fleet, Routing, Control Plane, Runtime, Observability, SRE

Data sources / implementation notes
- Primary source: incident-bot events + incident timeline annotations emitted to metrics/log pipeline.
- Labels: incident_type, incident_subtype, severity, tier, primary_owner.
- Cardinality guardrails:
  - incident_type and incident_subtype must use allowlisted values only; unknown -> "unknown".
  - Do NOT add freeform service names as labels; use a low-card service_group where needed.
  - Ensure dashboards don’t accidentally query high-cardinality dimensions (e.g., incident_id) except in drill-down tables.

Validation plan
- Backtest against last 90 days once backfill is available (ENG-4827): compare counts to SRE incident list and postmortems.
- Spot-check 10 incidents across categories (routing, capacity, runtime regression, control plane outage, auth/RBAC, streaming disconnects) and verify:
  - incident_type present
  - owner matches control-plane mapping (ENG-4825)
  - mitigation/fix timestamps produce expected SLA outcome

Acceptance criteria
- Dashboards published in the shared folder and linked from the taxonomy Confluence page.
- Saved views created for Hosted/Dedicated/Private and for each primary owner team.
- “Unknown/unclassified” panel present and visible; weekly trend is measurable.
- Data parity: for the last 14 days, incident count on dashboard matches incident-bot channel count within ±5% (allowing for test incidents filtered out).
- Observability sign-off from Allison Grant on cardinality safety + query performance.

Rollout
- Soft launch to SRE + oncall leads for 1 week.
- After validation, announce in #eng-sre and #incidents with links and “how to interpret” notes.

Notes / open questions
- SLA outcome semantics: If mitigation occurs but fix not yet deployed, SLA compliance should reflect mitigation SLA (TTM) vs fix SLA (TTF) separately. Tie to ENG-4829 implementation details.
- Multi-cause incidents: dashboard uses primary incident_type; contributing factors tracked in postmortem only for v1 (avoid label explosion).

title: Define tiered SLIs + exclusions (success semantics, latency points, streaming rules)
preview:
Context
- We need canonical, tier-aware SLIs for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved) to support tiered SLO targets and error budget enforcement.
- This ticket defines *measurement boundaries* + *what counts* (and what is excluded) for success rate, latency, and streaming reliability across core API request paths.
- Primary motivation: customer escalations around latency spikes and 5xx / stream disconnects during burst traffic; Product SLAs need to map cleanly to engineered SLIs.

In scope endpoints (initial)
1) Chat/Completions (non-streaming)
2) Chat/Completions (streaming via SSE)
3) Embeddings (non-streaming)

Tiering
- SLIs MUST be computed per tier label (Hosted vs Dedicated) and region.
- Tier label is treated as authoritative based on request context set at ingress and propagated through routing/fallback (see dependency ENG-4922).

SLI #1: Request success rate (availability / error-rate SLI)
Definition
- Numerator: count of requests that are considered successful.
- Denominator: count of requests that are considered valid + admitted to the system (i.e., not excluded).

Success criteria (counts as success)
- HTTP status in {200, 201, 202, 204} for non-streaming endpoints.
- For streaming endpoints:
  - Stream opened successfully (initial headers sent) AND
  - Terminal "[DONE]" / equivalent end-of-stream marker emitted OR server-side terminal event emitted (e.g., "stream.completed") with no server error.

Failure criteria (counts against success rate)
- Any 5xx returned by gateway/control plane/router/model server.
- Any explicit server-side stream error event ("stream.error") regardless of whether partial tokens were sent.
- Stream disconnect before terminal marker where disconnect is attributed to server-side fault (see streaming rules below).

Exclusions (removed from denominator)
- All 4xx that indicate invalid or unauthenticated/unauthorized requests (e.g., 400, 401, 403, 404, 405, 415, 422).
- 429 (rate limited) and 408 (request timeout prior to admission) are excluded from availability SLI for this phase; tracked separately as an overload signal.
- Client-aborted requests where the client closes the connection (classified as 499 at gateway or equivalent signal) are excluded.
- Internal health checks / synthetic probes that are not representative of customer traffic (must be tagged user_agent/probe and excluded).

Important: retries and fallbacks
- Retries: SLI is based on the final outcome of the logical request as seen by the gateway request id (if we only have per-attempt metrics today, we must document that limitation and ensure recording rules dedupe at aggregation time).
- Fallback routing (model/region/capacity tier) does NOT exclude the request. If it succeeds, it is success; if it fails, it is failure.
- We will add a dimension/annotation (fallback=true/false) for analysis, but the SLI must be computable without exploding cardinality (no per-customer/per-model by default).

SLI #2: End-to-end latency
We will publish 2 latency families to avoid ambiguity:
A) "Gateway E2E" latency (primary SLO latency SLI)
- Non-streaming: time from gateway request received (t_ingress) to gateway response fully sent (t_last_byte).
- Streaming: time from gateway request received (t_ingress) to first byte of stream (TTFB) AND stream duration (t_done - t_first_byte). For SLO purposes we initially target TTFB; stream duration is monitored but not a gate in the first enforcement phase.

B) "Model service" latency (secondary diagnostic SLI)
- Time from router/model-server handoff to model-server response completion (non-streaming) or to first token (streaming), for debugging/ownership. Not used for tier SLO compliance.

Latency exclusions
- Exclude requests that are excluded from availability denominator (invalid 4xx, 429, client aborts).
- For Dedicated: if a request is rejected due to explicit capacity admission control before model dispatch (e.g., shed/overload response), it is excluded from latency SLI but still tracked as an overload metric; this is a policy lever that will be constrained by Product/SRE (we must not “game” latency by shedding excessively).

Latency bucketing / percentiles
- Report p50/p95/p99 by tier x region x endpoint class.
- Windowing: 30d rolling for SLO reporting; 6h/1h windows for burn-rate style latency regressions (implementation in alerting ticket).

SLI #3: Streaming reliability
Goal
- Make stream disconnects and partial responses first-class and consistently attributed.

Events/metrics required
- stream.started (headers flushed)
- stream.first_token (optional but useful)
- stream.completed (terminal marker emitted)
- stream.error (server emitted error)
- stream.client_abort (best-effort classification from gateway TCP close / context cancellation)

Streaming reliability SLI
- Denominator: streams started (stream.started) for valid/admitted requests.
- Numerator: streams completed successfully (stream.completed) OR equivalent "[DONE]" observed at gateway.
- Failures: stream.error OR disconnect prior to completion where client_abort=false.

Attribution rules
- If gateway observes client close/cancel and we can classify as client_abort=true, exclude from denominator (consistent with availability exclusions).
- If disconnect happens without client_abort evidence and without successful completion marker, count as failure.
- If server emits stream.error after sending partial tokens, count as failure.

Regional routing / failover interactions
- If router fails over mid-request (rare; should generally not happen for in-flight streaming), treat as a single logical stream if request id preserved. If the platform today cannot preserve identity across mid-stream failover, count each started stream attempt separately and document as a known measurement limitation.

Cardinality / tagging constraints (must-have)
- SLI computations must be possible with labels limited to:
  - tier (hosted|dedicated)
  - region
  - endpoint_class (chat_nonstream|chat_stream|embeddings)
  - status_class (2xx|4xx|5xx|client_abort|rate_limited)
  - optional: fallback (true|false)
- Avoid per-model and per-customer in base SLI time series; those belong in drill-down dashboards with controlled queries/recording rules.

Deliverables
1) Confluence-ready definitions (formulas + exclusions + measurement points) to be copied into the shared standards doc.
2) Open questions resolved + documented (esp. 429 handling, overload shedding, streaming attribution).
3) Hand-off notes for ENG-4926 (SLO config schema) to ensure schema can express endpoint_class, tier, region, and the exclusion filters.

Open questions (resolved in this ticket)
- 429 handling: exclude from availability SLI, track separately; revisit once admission control policies settle.
- Streaming SLO basis: use TTFB for latency SLO; monitor stream duration and completion rate as separate SLI.
- Client aborts: exclude when clearly attributable; ambiguous disconnects count against us.

Notes / known limitations
- If we lack a stable request id across retries, early SLI may slightly over-count failures due to double-counting attempts. This must be called out until gateway recording rules can aggregate by request id.

Owners
- SLI definitions + documentation: Aisha Bello (SRE)
- Gateway instrumentation alignment: Amara Diallo
- Routing attribution alignment: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
- Telemetry constraints review: Logan Wright / Thomas Baird
text:
Context
- We need canonical, tier-aware SLIs for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved) to support tiered SLO targets and error budget enforcement.
- This ticket defines *measurement boundaries* + *what counts* (and what is excluded) for success rate, latency, and streaming reliability across core API request paths.
- Primary motivation: customer escalations around latency spikes and 5xx / stream disconnects during burst traffic; Product SLAs need to map cleanly to engineered SLIs.

In scope endpoints (initial)
1) Chat/Completions (non-streaming)
2) Chat/Completions (streaming via SSE)
3) Embeddings (non-streaming)

Tiering
- SLIs MUST be computed per tier label (Hosted vs Dedicated) and region.
- Tier label is treated as authoritative based on request context set at ingress and propagated through routing/fallback (see dependency ENG-4922).

SLI #1: Request success rate (availability / error-rate SLI)
Definition
- Numerator: count of requests that are considered successful.
- Denominator: count of requests that are considered valid + admitted to the system (i.e., not excluded).

Success criteria (counts as success)
- HTTP status in {200, 201, 202, 204} for non-streaming endpoints.
- For streaming endpoints:
  - Stream opened successfully (initial headers sent) AND
  - Terminal "[DONE]" / equivalent end-of-stream marker emitted OR server-side terminal event emitted (e.g., "stream.completed") with no server error.

Failure criteria (counts against success rate)
- Any 5xx returned by gateway/control plane/router/model server.
- Any explicit server-side stream error event ("stream.error") regardless of whether partial tokens were sent.
- Stream disconnect before terminal marker where disconnect is attributed to server-side fault (see streaming rules below).

Exclusions (removed from denominator)
- All 4xx that indicate invalid or unauthenticated/unauthorized requests (e.g., 400, 401, 403, 404, 405, 415, 422).
- 429 (rate limited) and 408 (request timeout prior to admission) are excluded from availability SLI for this phase; tracked separately as an overload signal.
- Client-aborted requests where the client closes the connection (classified as 499 at gateway or equivalent signal) are excluded.
- Internal health checks / synthetic probes that are not representative of customer traffic (must be tagged user_agent/probe and excluded).

Important: retries and fallbacks
- Retries: SLI is based on the final outcome of the logical request as seen by the gateway request id (if we only have per-attempt metrics today, we must document that limitation and ensure recording rules dedupe at aggregation time).
- Fallback routing (model/region/capacity tier) does NOT exclude the request. If it succeeds, it is success; if it fails, it is failure.
- We will add a dimension/annotation (fallback=true/false) for analysis, but the SLI must be computable without exploding cardinality (no per-customer/per-model by default).

SLI #2: End-to-end latency
We will publish 2 latency families to avoid ambiguity:
A) "Gateway E2E" latency (primary SLO latency SLI)
- Non-streaming: time from gateway request received (t_ingress) to gateway response fully sent (t_last_byte).
- Streaming: time from gateway request received (t_ingress) to first byte of stream (TTFB) AND stream duration (t_done - t_first_byte). For SLO purposes we initially target TTFB; stream duration is monitored but not a gate in the first enforcement phase.

B) "Model service" latency (secondary diagnostic SLI)
- Time from router/model-server handoff to model-server response completion (non-streaming) or to first token (streaming), for debugging/ownership. Not used for tier SLO compliance.

Latency exclusions
- Exclude requests that are excluded from availability denominator (invalid 4xx, 429, client aborts).
- For Dedicated: if a request is rejected due to explicit capacity admission control before model dispatch (e.g., shed/overload response), it is excluded from latency SLI but still tracked as an overload metric; this is a policy lever that will be constrained by Product/SRE (we must not “game” latency by shedding excessively).

Latency bucketing / percentiles
- Report p50/p95/p99 by tier x region x endpoint class.
- Windowing: 30d rolling for SLO reporting; 6h/1h windows for burn-rate style latency regressions (implementation in alerting ticket).

SLI #3: Streaming reliability
Goal
- Make stream disconnects and partial responses first-class and consistently attributed.

Events/metrics required
- stream.started (headers flushed)
- stream.first_token (optional but useful)
- stream.completed (terminal marker emitted)
- stream.error (server emitted error)
- stream.client_abort (best-effort classification from gateway TCP close / context cancellation)

Streaming reliability SLI
- Denominator: streams started (stream.started) for valid/admitted requests.
- Numerator: streams completed successfully (stream.completed) OR equivalent "[DONE]" observed at gateway.
- Failures: stream.error OR disconnect prior to completion where client_abort=false.

Attribution rules
- If gateway observes client close/cancel and we can classify as client_abort=true, exclude from denominator (consistent with availability exclusions).
- If disconnect happens without client_abort evidence and without successful completion marker, count as failure.
- If server emits stream.error after sending partial tokens, count as failure.

Regional routing / failover interactions
- If router fails over mid-request (rare; should generally not happen for in-flight streaming), treat as a single logical stream if request id preserved. If the platform today cannot preserve identity across mid-stream failover, count each started stream attempt separately and document as a known measurement limitation.

Cardinality / tagging constraints (must-have)
- SLI computations must be possible with labels limited to:
  - tier (hosted|dedicated)
  - region
  - endpoint_class (chat_nonstream|chat_stream|embeddings)
  - status_class (2xx|4xx|5xx|client_abort|rate_limited)
  - optional: fallback (true|false)
- Avoid per-model and per-customer in base SLI time series; those belong in drill-down dashboards with controlled queries/recording rules.

Deliverables
1) Confluence-ready definitions (formulas + exclusions + measurement points) to be copied into the shared standards doc.
2) Open questions resolved + documented (esp. 429 handling, overload shedding, streaming attribution).
3) Hand-off notes for ENG-4926 (SLO config schema) to ensure schema can express endpoint_class, tier, region, and the exclusion filters.

Open questions (resolved in this ticket)
- 429 handling: exclude from availability SLI, track separately; revisit once admission control policies settle.
- Streaming SLO basis: use TTFB for latency SLO; monitor stream duration and completion rate as separate SLI.
- Client aborts: exclude when clearly attributable; ambiguous disconnects count against us.

Notes / known limitations
- If we lack a stable request id across retries, early SLI may slightly over-count failures due to double-counting attempts. This must be called out until gateway recording rules can aggregate by request id.

Owners
- SLI definitions + documentation: Aisha Bello (SRE)
- Gateway instrumentation alignment: Amara Diallo
- Routing attribution alignment: Mina Haddad / Ravi Menon
- Telemetry constraints review: Logan Wright / Thomas Baird

title: Regional failover automation upgrade (Hosted API + Dedicated) — umbrella
preview:
## Context
Today, regional failover is a mix of manual actions and partially automated routing policies. Detection can be noisy (false positives), real regional impairment can be slow to identify, and end-to-end safety validation is hard.

This umbrella tracks the Reliability Program work to:
- Improve trigger signal pipeline (reachability, error burn, tail latency burn, capacity headroom, network symptoms)
- Add guardrails + deterministic policy evaluation (avoid flapping/oscillation)
- Validate via controlled failover tests (“game days”) with measurable SLO outcomes
- Improve observability so on-call can quickly distinguish region impairment vs local service issues

## Goals (success criteria)
- Detection + failover time reduced vs baseline (target: automated recommendation within minutes; execution gated by safety limits/optional approval hooks)
- Fewer false-positive regional triggers (measured by alert-to-action ratio + post-event audits)
- No cascading overload during failover (gateway retry budgets + load shedding tuned)
- Repeatable game day plan + results, with follow-ups tracked and closed
- Clear audit trail (who/what/why) for automated recommendations, executions, and rollbacks

## Scope
In-scope:
- Hosted API + Dedicated multi-region routing + policy evaluation
- Region health aggregation service + controller
- Circuit breakers/load shedding behavior during failover
- Dashboards/alerts/runbooks
- Incident-bot + status comms hooks
- Controlled tests in staging + production (limited blast radius)

Out of scope:
- Multi-cloud migration
- Rewriting core serving runtime
- Contract/SLA language changes

## Ownership / RACI (high level)
- Program: Sean Gallagher (Program Lead), Rafael Mendes (Delivery Lead), Talia Benson (PgM)
- Failover architecture + guardrails: Miles Thornton
- Routing + policy engine: Mina Haddad (Eng Lead), Ravi Menon (Tech Lead), Jordan Lee (Policy engine owner)
- Control plane integration: Ethan Park
- Capacity signals + autoscaling gates: Vanessa Ortiz, Keiko Yamamoto
- Network reachability signals: Victor Alvarez, Emily Zhao
- Observability: Allison Grant (Lead), Sergio Costa (Dashboards/alerts), Aisha Bello (SLO dashboards)
- Gateway overload/circuit breakers: Peter Holtz
- Tooling integrations (incident-bot): Devon Price
- Runbooks/docs: Tomáš Novák, Kira Thompson, Elliot Price

## Milestones (Q1–Q2 2026)
### Phase 1 — Baseline + requirements (complete)
- Collect current failover behavior + top incident failure modes
- Define trigger hierarchy draft + initial SLO targets

### Phase 2 — Design (in progress)
- ADR-042: trigger signal priority + corroboration + dampening/hold-down timers
- ADR-043: rollback semantics + anti-flap + audit requirements
- Draft runbooks + controlled test plan

### Phase 3 — Build (in progress)
- Region health aggregator service + caching/TTL + integration points
- Failover controller with dry-run mode + optional approval hooks
- Capacity + network reachability inputs wired into decisioning
- SLO-friendly metrics + burn alerts + dashboards + alert annotations
- Gateway timeouts/retry budgets tuned; standardized retry-after responses

### Phase 4 — Validate (planned)
- Staging drills using config presets + simulation endpoint
- Controlled production game day (explicit go/no-go, limited blast radius)
- Success criteria: failover time, 5xx rate, p95/p99 latency, error budget burn, customer impact checks

### Phase 5 — Rollout + hardening (planned)
- Expand enablement to additional routes/models/tiers via feature flags
- Close gaps found in game days; finalize docs/runbooks
- Establish quarterly failover test cadence + automatic reporting

## Key artifacts / links
Confluence / docs:
- sources/confluence/eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/regional-failover-automation-upgrade-overview.json
- sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/multi-region-availability-slo-2026.json
- sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-042-failover-trigger-signal-hierarchy.json
- sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-043-automated-routing-policy-rollback.json
- sources/confluence/eng-platform/systems-and-services/region-health-service.json
- sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook.json
- sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook.json
- sources/confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure.json
- sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/failover-signal-dashboards-spec.json
- sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/controlled-failover-test-plan-q1-2026.json
- sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process/regional-failover-communication-playbook.json
- sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comms/status-page-template-regional-event.json
- sources/confluence/security-and-compliance/incident-response-security/failover-and-security-boundaries.json

Related PRs (tracking only; individual issues own implementation details):
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19810-add-region-health-aggregator-service.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19812-policy-engine-signal-priority.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19814-failover-controller-dry-run.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19815-routing-policy-rollback-safety.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19816-add-multi-region-slo-metrics.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19818-alerts-failover-burn-rate.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19820-api-gateway-fallback-timeout-tuning.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19821-streaming-reconnect-on-redirect.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19823-integrate-capacity-signal-to-routing.json
- sources/github/redwood/pr-19824-failover-event-audit-log.json

## Notes / open questions
- Final stance for production: approval hooks required for execution, or only for first N weeks? (see ENG-2415/ENG-2416)
- “Do not fail over” scenarios: partial impairments (single AZ), control plane degradation, noisy network symptoms.
- Data residency boundaries for enterprise tiers: ensure automation respects constraints by policy. (coordinate with security guidance)
- Metric label cardinality limits for reason codes + tenant segmentation; keep dashboards useful without exploding TSDB.

## How to use this umbrella
- Keep cross-team milestones + status here.
- Implementation work stays in sub-issues.
- Any scope change requires updating: Goals, Milestones, and Go/No-Go criteria for the next game day.
