title: Perf canary: alerting policy + on-call routing (warn/page) and incident-bot integration
preview:
Goal
Define and implement production alert rules + routing for the perf regression canary (per-model), including incident-bot formatting/dedupe, and explicit anti-flap/noise-budget requirements.

Context
We are deploying an always-on synthetic canary in production to detect p95 latency regressions and tokens/sec drops per model, with attribution signals (queueing vs runtime execution, kernel selection tags, runtime build tags).

This ticket covers:
- Warning vs paging thresholds and when they apply (top models vs all models)
- Required anti-flap: min sample size, sustained windows, dampening, cooldowns
- Notification routing: #eng-runtime vs #eng-oncall, plus escalation matrix
- Incident-bot integration: payload fields, dedupe keys, thread formatting, and links to dashboards/runbook
- Noise budget: maximum pages/warnings per day/week, plus what to tune when exceeded

Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Building the canary service itself (ENG-4872)
- Creating dashboards/panels (ENG-4877)
- Writing full runbook (ENG-4883) — but we define the contract: what links/fields must exist

Proposed alert taxonomy (initial)
We will ship two alert levels initially:

1) Warning (non-paging)
- Purpose: early detection and triage signal for runtime team; should be sensitive but not noisy.
- Route: #eng-runtime (no page)
- Coverage: all canary-covered models

2) Paging (Sev2 default)
- Purpose: sustained/regression that is likely customer-impacting on a top model / tier.
- Route: on-call rotation via incident-bot + #eng-oncall; also notify #eng-runtime
- Coverage: top models only (phase 1); later expand once noise is stable.

We also define an “info-only” event (no alert) for baseline missing / bootstrap mode. That should surface in dashboard annotations and logs but not notify Slack.

Signals and definitions
Primary signals (per model, per region, per hardware SKU if configured):
- p95 end-to-end latency (ms)
- p95 runtime-only latency (ms) (to separate network/control plane)
- tokens/sec (throughput)
Secondary attribution signals (should be attached in alert context, not necessarily alerted on):
- p95 scheduler queueing time (ms)
- p95 prefill latency (ms)
- p95 decode latency (ms)
- batch size distribution (e.g., avg/percentiles)
- KV cache hit rate (if applicable)
- runtime build id + kernel selection id tags

Baseline + regression math (policy-level contract; implementation details in ENG-4874 / ADR)
- Baseline key must include: model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hardware_sku (+ region if enabled)
- Regression is expressed as percent deviation from baseline median/p95 (depending on metric)
- Minimum sample sizes required before an alert can fire (to prevent “one bad batch”)

Anti-flap requirements (must-have)
- min_samples: Do not evaluate until >= N requests in the evaluation window.
- sustained_window: regression must persist for >= M minutes (or >= K consecutive windows).
- dampening: once fired, do not re-fire for cooldown period unless severity increases.
- dedupe: incident-bot must dedupe by (alert_type, model_id, model_version, region, hardware_sku, runtime_build) with a time-bucketed key.
- missing-data handling: missing telemetry should not be treated as regression; alert separately only if we decide to (not in phase 1).

Initial thresholds (phase 1; tune during us-east rollout)
Warning alerts (all covered models)
- Latency p95 e2e: +10% vs baseline for 15m, min_samples >= 300
- Latency p95 runtime-only: +10% vs baseline for 15m, min_samples >= 300
- Tokens/sec: -8% vs baseline for 15m, min_samples >= 300

Paging alerts (top models only)
- Latency p95 e2e: +15% vs baseline for 10m, min_samples >= 500
- Tokens/sec: -12% vs baseline for 10m, min_samples >= 500

Escalation to Sev1 (rare; phase 1 optional)
- Latency p95 e2e: +25% vs baseline for 20m on a top model AND impact spans >= 2 regions OR coincides with active rollout gate.

Notes
- Thresholds are intentionally asymmetric (tokens/sec more sensitive to batching/fleet mix); expect tuning after week 1.
- Region/SKU-specific thresholds are allowed but must be explicit in config (avoid implicit per-region divergence that becomes unmaintainable).

Noise budget (operational targets)
- Paging: <= 2 pages/week for perf-canary in phase 1 (us-east only); if exceeded, we must pause expansion and tune thresholds/baselines.
- Warnings: <= 5 warnings/day per region across all models (aggregate). If exceeded, tune min_samples/sustained_window first before raising thresholds.
- Any single model generating > 3 warnings/day should be flagged for “model-specific threshold” review.

Incident-bot integration requirements
Alert payload must include (at minimum):
- model_id, model_version
- region, tier (if applicable), hardware_sku
- runtime_build_id, kernel_selection_id (if available)
- metric_name, baseline_value, observed_value, percent_change
- evaluation_window, min_samples, observed_samples
- confidence/scoring (if available) or “reason” string (e.g., sustained 3/3 windows)
- links:
  - Grafana dashboard deep link filtered to model/region/SKU
  - runbook link: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert.json
  - recent runtime release notes (internal) if runtime_build changed (best effort)

Slack formatting
- Post a single message per dedupe key into #eng-runtime for warnings.
- For paging: open a thread in #incidents (or #eng-oncall depending on current standard) and cross-post a compact summary to #eng-runtime.
- Incident-bot must annotate whether this alert is currently gating a rollout (phase 2).

Routing matrix (phase 1)
- Warning: #eng-runtime (owner: Serving Runtime). FYI @telemetry if pipeline lag suspected.
- Page: On-call (SRE primary) + runtime secondary. SRE leads incident workflow; runtime provides RCA/rollback recommendation.

Open questions
1) Do we page SRE or runtime directly for paging alerts? Current proposal: page SRE primary due to incident process + comms; runtime is secondary.
2) Where do we land the “missing telemetry / pipeline delay” signal? (separate SLO for canary health vs perf regression)
3) Do we allow per-model per-region thresholds in config at launch, or gate behind “exception process” to avoid config sprawl?

Links / references
- Dashboard spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/perf-canary-dashboards-spec.json
- SLO policy mapping: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/per-model-latency-slos-and-alerting-policy.json

Acceptance criteria
- Warning alert rule exists for p95 latency and tokens/sec regression with:
  - min_samples and sustained_window enforced
  - routes to #eng-runtime
  - includes dashboard + runbook links
- Paging alert rule exists for top-model set with:
  - stronger threshold and sustained_window
  - routes to on-call via incident-bot
  - dedupe + cooldown prevents alert storms
- Incident-bot formats alerts into consistent Slack messages/threads with required fields and stable dedupe key.
- Noise budget documented and agreed by SRE + runtime; tuning plan documented for week 1.

Rollout plan (this ticket)
- Phase 1: us-east, 2–3 models, warnings enabled day 1; paging enabled only after 48–72h of stable warning volume and baseline sanity checks.

text:
Goal
Define and implement production alert rules + routing for the perf regression canary (per-model), including incident-bot formatting/dedupe, and explicit anti-flap/noise-budget requirements.

Context
We are deploying an always-on synthetic canary in production to detect p95 latency regressions and tokens/sec drops per model, with attribution signals (queueing vs runtime execution, kernel selection tags, runtime build tags).

This ticket covers:
- Warning vs paging thresholds and when they apply (top models vs all models)
- Required anti-flap: min sample size, sustained windows, dampening, cooldowns
- Notification routing: #eng-runtime vs #eng-oncall, plus escalation matrix
- Incident-bot integration: payload fields, dedupe keys, thread formatting, and links to dashboards/runbook
- Noise budget: maximum pages/warnings per day/week, plus what to tune when exceeded

Non-goals (for this ticket)
- Building the canary service itself (ENG-4872)
- Creating dashboards/panels (ENG-4877)
- Writing full runbook (ENG-4883) — but we define the contract: what links/fields must exist

Proposed alert taxonomy (initial)
We will ship two alert levels initially:

1) Warning (non-paging)
- Purpose: early detection and triage signal for runtime team; should be sensitive but not noisy.
- Route: #eng-runtime (no page)
- Coverage: all canary-covered models

2) Paging (Sev2 default)
- Purpose: sustained/regression that is likely customer-impacting on a top model / tier.
- Route: on-call rotation via incident-bot + #eng-oncall; also notify #eng-runtime
- Coverage: top models only (phase 1); later expand once noise is stable.

We also define an “info-only” event (no alert) for baseline missing / bootstrap mode. That should surface in dashboard annotations and logs but not notify Slack.

Signals and definitions
Primary signals (per model, per region, per hardware SKU if configured):
- p95 end-to-end latency (ms)
- p95 runtime-only latency (ms) (to separate network/control plane)
- tokens/sec (throughput)
Secondary attribution signals (should be attached in alert context, not necessarily alerted on):
- p95 scheduler queueing time (ms)
- p95 prefill latency (ms)
- p95 decode latency (ms)
- batch size distribution (e.g., avg/percentiles)
- KV cache hit rate (if applicable)
- runtime build id + kernel selection id tags

Baseline + regression math (policy-level contract; implementation details in ENG-4874 / ADR)
- Baseline key must include: model_id + model_version + runtime_build + hardware_sku (+ region if enabled)
- Regression is expressed as percent deviation from baseline median/p95 (depending on metric)
- Minimum sample sizes required before an alert can fire (to prevent “one bad batch”)

Anti-flap requirements (must-have)
- min_samples: Do not evaluate until >= N requests in the evaluation window.
- sustained_window: regression must persist for >= M minutes (or >= K consecutive windows).
- dampening: once fired, do not re-fire for cooldown period unless severity increases.
- dedupe: incident-bot must dedupe by (alert_type, model_id, model_version, region, hardware_sku, runtime_build) with a time-bucketed key.
- missing-data handling: missing telemetry should not be treated as regression; alert separately only if we decide to (not in phase 1).

Initial thresholds (phase 1; tune during us-east rollout)
Warning alerts (all covered models)
- Latency p95 e2e: +10% vs baseline for 15m, min_samples >= 300
- Latency p95 runtime-only: +10% vs baseline for 15m, min_samples >= 300
- Tokens/sec: -8% vs baseline for 15m, min_samples >= 300

Paging alerts (top models only)
- Latency p95 e2e: +15% vs baseline for 10m, min_samples >= 500
- Tokens/sec: -12% vs baseline for 10m, min_samples >= 500

Escalation to Sev1 (rare; phase 1 optional)
- Latency p95 e2e: +25% vs baseline for 20m on a top model AND impact spans >= 2 regions OR coincides with active rollout gate.

Notes
- Thresholds are intentionally asymmetric (tokens/sec more sensitive to batching/fleet mix); expect tuning after week 1.
- Region/SKU-specific thresholds are allowed but must be explicit in config (avoid implicit per-region divergence that becomes unmaintainable).

Noise budget (operational targets)
- Paging: <= 2 pages/week for perf-canary in phase 1 (us-east only); if exceeded, we must pause expansion and tune thresholds/baselines.
- Warnings: <= 5 warnings/day per region across all models (aggregate). If exceeded, tune min_samples/sustained_window first before raising thresholds.
- Any single model generating > 3 warnings/day should be flagged for “model-specific threshold” review.

Incident-bot integration requirements
Alert payload must include (at minimum):
- model_id, model_version
- region, tier (if applicable), hardware_sku
- runtime_build_id, kernel_selection_id (if available)
- metric_name, baseline_value, observed_value, percent_change
- evaluation_window, min_samples, observed_samples
- confidence/scoring (if available) or “reason” string (e.g., sustained 3/3 windows)
- links:
  - Grafana dashboard deep link filtered to model/region/SKU
  - runbook link: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/runbook-perf-regression-canary-alert.json
  - recent runtime release notes (internal) if runtime_build changed (best effort)

Slack formatting
- Post a single message per dedupe key into #eng-runtime for warnings.
- For paging: open a thread in #incidents (or #eng-oncall depending on current standard) and cross-post a compact summary to #eng-runtime.
- Incident-bot must annotate whether this alert is currently gating a rollout (phase 2).

Routing matrix (phase 1)
- Warning: #eng-runtime (owner: Serving Runtime). FYI @telemetry if pipeline lag suspected.
- Page: On-call (SRE primary) + runtime secondary. SRE leads incident workflow; runtime provides RCA/rollback recommendation.

Open questions
1) Do we page SRE or runtime directly for paging alerts? Current proposal: page SRE primary due to incident process + comms; runtime is secondary.
2) Where do we land the “missing telemetry / pipeline delay” signal? (separate SLO for canary health vs perf regression)
3) Do we allow per-model per-region thresholds in config at launch, or gate behind “exception process” to avoid config sprawl?

Links / references
- Dashboard spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/perf-canary-dashboards-spec.json
- SLO policy mapping: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/per-model-latency-slos-and-alerting-policy.json
- Baselines ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-perf-canary-baseline-and-thresholds.json
- Incident-bot handler PR (expected): sources/github/incident-bot/pr-18603-add-perf-canary-alert-handler.json
- Observability alert rules PRs (expected):
  - sources/github/observability-pack/pr-19179-perf-canary-alert-rules-warning.json
  - sources/github/observability-pack/pr-19183-perf-canary-alert-rules-paging.json

Acceptance criteria
- Warning alert rule exists for p95 latency and tokens/sec regression with:
  - min_samples and sustained_window enforced
  - routes to #eng-runtime
  - includes dashboard + runbook links
- Paging alert rule exists for top-model set with:
  - stronger threshold and sustained_window
  - routes to on-call via incident-bot
  - dedupe + cooldown prevents alert storms
- Incident-bot formats alerts into consistent Slack messages/threads with required fields and stable dedupe key.
- Noise budget documented and agreed by SRE + runtime; tuning plan documented for week 1.

Rollout plan (this ticket)
- Phase 1: us-east, 2–3 models, warnings enabled day 1; paging enabled only after 48–72h of stable warning volume and baseline sanity checks.
- Phase 1 exit criteria: <= 1 page/week, warnings stable, no recurring false-positive driver.

Owner notes
Rafael owns routing/paging standards + incident-bot behavior. Allison owns alert policy consistency with broader observability standards and ensures the alert payload includes actionable links.

title: Runbook refresh: regional failover + Dedicated drain procedures aligned to new automation/rollback
preview:
Why
- Rehearsals + Game Day 1 surfaced that existing runbooks still describe manual failover steps and don’t match the new controller behavior (dry-run, approvals, anti-flap timers, rollback semantics, per-tenant safety limits).
- Dedicated drain guidance also needs to reflect new sequencing/guardrails so we don’t overload the target region or strand dedicated workloads.

Scope (this ticket)
Update the on-call runbooks + operator guide entry points so they reflect the new end-to-end workflow:
1) Detect/confirm regional impairment using standardized signals (reachability > 5xx burn > tail latency burn > capacity).
2) Use failover controller in dry-run to preview recommended actions + evidence.
3) Execute with approval hooks (prod), respecting rate limits/anti-flap and per-tenant safety constraints.
4) Validate traffic shift + customer impact + SLO burn.
5) Drain/evacuate GPU pools (Hosted + Dedicated) using the updated sequencing.
6) Roll back or hold-down, with clear criteria to avoid oscillation.
7) Ensure audit events + timeline annotations are captured for post-incident review.

Primary docs to update (Confluence)
- Routing Policy Failover Controller Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook.json
- Hosted API Regional Failover Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook.json
- GPU Pool Drain and Evacuation Procedure: sources/confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure.json
- Regional Failover Communication Playbook (cross-link + “when to notify Support”): sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process/regional-failover-communication-playbook.json

Must align with
- ADR-042 Failover Trigger Signal Hierarchy: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-042-failover-trigger-signal-hierarchy.json
- ADR-043 Automated Routing Policy Rollback: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-043-automated-routing-policy-rollback.json
- Failover dashboards spec (link + panel reading tips): sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/failover-signal-dashboards-spec.json
- Failover controller CLI design (operator commands + output expectations): sources/confluence/eng-sre/operational-tooling/failover-controller-cli-design.json

Key deltas to document explicitly
- Dry-run mode is the default first step (must capture “recommended actions”, “confidence”, and “evidence signals”).
- “Do NOT fail over” scenarios (e.g., localized dependency outage, single-AZ impairment masked by routing, control plane partial degradation).
- Anti-flap / hold-down timers: what they do, how they present in logs, and how to interpret an automated rollback.
- Per-tenant safety limits: max flips/hour, blast radius constraints; what happens when enforcement blocks an action.
- Dedicated drain workflow: gating on target region headroom + drain rate; explicit coordination points with routing.
- Overload + circuit breakers: expected 429/503 + Retry-After behavior during failover; guidance for Support/client comms.
- Audit trail: where to find failover audit events, how to attach to incident timeline, retention expectations for enterprise asks.

Owners / reviewers
- Primary: Tomáš Novák (runbooks + paging hygiene)
- Contributor: Kira Thompson (runbooks/docs writer)
- Reviewers: Miles Thornton (architecture), Mina Haddad (routing), Peter Holtz (gateway/circuit breakers), Allison Grant (observability), Irene Choi (infra/drain)

Links
- Umbrella: ENG-2411
- Related PRs: redwood-docs PR-982 (operator guide), redwood PR-19824 (audit log), PR-19814 (dry-run), PR-19815 (rollback safety), PR-19822/19842 (dedicated drain + metrics)

Notes from Game Day 1 that this runbook refresh must address
- Confusion on who “approves” automation vs who “executes” during prod events.
- Dashboards lacked consistent annotations for automated flips; on-call spent time correlating timelines.
- Dedicated drain steps were missing target-region headroom checks; needed a clear go/no-go gate.
- Streaming disconnect behavior created noisy client-side retries; runbook should call out expected symptoms and mitigations.

title: Dedicated pool drain automation for regional failover workflows (with safety gates)
preview:
Context
Dedicated customers run on reserved GPU pools with isolation requirements; when a region is impaired or we execute a controlled failover, we need a safe, repeatable way to drain / evacuate Dedicated pools without causing cascading overload, thrashing routing policies, or violating per-tenant availability assumptions.

Today (baseline)
- Drain actions are manual / semi-manual (a mix of ad-hoc scripts + operator judgement).
- Sequencing is not standardized: routing flips sometimes happen before pools are actually able to shed load, leading to transient 5xx/timeouts and noisy alerts.
- Limited guardrails: no hard limits on how much traffic is being displaced concurrently, and limited visibility into drain progress.

Goal
Automate Dedicated pool drain and evacuation as a first-class step in the regional failover workflow, with explicit safety gates and observability. This should work for:
- Planned controlled failover tests (“game days”) with a limited blast radius.
- Real regional impairment mitigations where Dedicated traffic must be shifted to a backup region / capacity tier.

Non-goals
- Full cross-cloud evacuation (out of scope for this program).
- Customer-specific bespoke automation for Private deployments.

Proposed approach
1) Introduce a dedicated drain workflow endpoint / controller action
- Add an internal control-plane endpoint to initiate and monitor drains for a Dedicated pool (per tenant / per pool).
- The workflow transitions through states with explicit invariants:
  - Requested → Validating → Draining → Evacuating → Completed (or RolledBack / Aborted)
- Drain = stop accepting new sessions/requests on the pool while allowing in-flight requests to complete up to configured limits.
- Evacuate = actively move routing weight away (or mark pool as ineligible) and enforce that new traffic does not land on the pool.

2) Safety gates (hard stops)
- Capacity headroom gate (target region): must meet configured headroom threshold for the tenant tier before evacuation proceeds (uses capacity signal work).
- Region health corroboration gate: only proceed if region impairment is corroborated OR if the change is explicitly marked as “planned test” with approved allowlist.
- Blast radius gate: limit concurrent drains (global) and per-tenant drain frequency (ties into per-tenant safety limits / anti-flap work).
- In-flight protection gate: do not forcibly terminate streams unless the region is fully unreachable; otherwise respect a maximum drain window and then switch to controlled shed with retry-after.

3) Integration points
- Routing policy engine: treat draining pools as “ineligible” destinations while drain is active; ensure deterministic policy evaluation.
- Failover controller: orchestrates ordering:
  (a) preflight checks → (b) start drain → (c) verify drain progress → (d) shift routing weight → (e) validate SLO signals → (f) close out and log.
- Incident-bot/audit logs: emit structured events at each state transition (who/what/why + evidence links).

4) Observability
- Metrics for drain lifecycle and progress:
  - dedicated_drain_active{region,pool_id,tier}
  - dedicated_drain_state{state,...}
  - dedicated_drain_inflight_requests{...}
  - dedicated_drain_duration_seconds{...}
- Dashboard annotations on routing flips and drain state transitions.

Acceptance criteria
- Operator can initiate a drain for a Dedicated pool via internal endpoint/CLI; can query status and see progress.
- Automation blocks on missing safety gates (capacity/headroom, blast radius, approvals), with clear error messages.
- Drain + evacuation sequencing is correct under load: traffic shifts away without triggering large retry storms or route oscillation.
- Rollback path exists:
  - abort drain restores eligibility and routing weight subject to anti-flap rules.
- Metrics + dashboard panels exist; incident-bot summary posts a concise state-change update.
- Staging rehearsal completed with at least one tenant allowlisted and a limited set of models/routes.

Testing plan
- Unit tests: state machine transitions; gating logic; idempotency on repeated “initiate drain” requests.
- Integration tests (staging):
  - Drain while serving steady load; verify in-flight completion behavior and drop in new traffic to draining pool.
  - Force capacity gate failure; ensure no routing shift occurs.
  - Abort mid-drain; verify restoration.
- Game day readiness: enable only for a small allowlist (Dedicated test tenants + one internal tenant) and one source/target region pair.

Risks / mitigations
- Risk: drain causes unexpected latency spikes due to client retry storms.
  - Mitigation: coordinate with gateway retry budgets + retry-after semantics; add explicit shed behaviors when draining.
- Risk: capacity signal is stale, leading to over-evacuation into a saturated region.
  - Mitigation: require corroboration + freshness window; hold-down timers.
- Risk: operator confusion / partial execution.
  - Mitigation: clear state machine visibility and runbook updates with exact decision points.

References / links
- Runbook: sources/confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure.json
- Program overview: sources/confluence/eng-sre/reliability-initiatives/regional-failover-automation-upgrade-overview.json
- Related PRs:
  - sources/github/redwood/pr-19822-dedicated-pool-drain-endpoint.json
  - sources/github/redwood/pr-19842-dedicated-drain-progress-metrics.json
- Related tickets:
  - ENG-2414 (capacity signal integration)
  - ENG-2416 (rollback guardrails)
  - ENG-2421 (game day execution)

Open questions
- What is the default max drain window for streaming requests in Dedicated? Proposed: 3–5 minutes per pool, then controlled shed with retry-after.
- Should planned tests bypass region-impairment corroboration entirely, or still require reachability probes to be healthy? (current proposal: planned tests still require probes healthy; only the “impairment required” check is bypassed with approval + allowlist).

Next steps
- Land endpoint + state machine behind feature flag.
- Add gating logic wired to capacity and region health services.
- Add progress metrics + dashboard annotations.
- Update runbooks and run a staging rehearsal prior to expanding to production allowlist.

- Should planned tests bypass region-impairment corroboration entirely, or still require reachability probes to be healthy? (current proposal: planned tests still require probes healthy; only the “impairment required” check is bypassed with approval + allowlist).

Next steps
- Land endpoint + state machine behind feature flag.
- Add gating logic wired to capacity and region health services.
- Add progress metrics + dashboard annotations.
- Update runbooks and run a staging rehearsal prior to expanding to production allowlist.

title: Fix streaming reconnect during region failover/redirect (idempotency + resume)
preview:
Problem
During regional impairment and automated routing flips, a non-trivial portion of streaming requests (SSE) are terminated mid-stream. Many clients treat this as a hard failure and re-issue the request, which can:
- Duplicate generation (double-charging tokens + duplicated assistant output)
- Increase thundering-herd behavior during failover windows
- Cause confusing UX for end-users (partial stream then restart)

We saw this clearly during staging rehearsals and in the 2026-01-15 controlled failover. Support also received reports of streaming disconnects during region redirect.

Scope
This issue is focused on Hosted API streaming behavior when a request is redirected cross-region (policy-driven failover) OR when the gateway returns overload/redirect responses during failover.

Observed symptoms
- SSE stream disconnects on redirect boundary; some clients do not follow redirects for streaming (or they follow but re-send without idempotency).
- Retries create a new request_id on the new region, so the server cannot safely dedupe.
- In some cases, the first region continues generating briefly after the redirect decision (wasted work + extra load).

Root cause (current behavior)
1) The API gateway may emit 307/308 Location redirects (or terminate upstream connections) while streaming is active.
2) Our streaming endpoints did not guarantee a stable, resumable stream identifier across regions.
3) SDK/client guidance did not strongly encourage idempotency keys for streaming requests, and our backend behavior was inconsistent when an idempotency key was present but the request was retried mid-stream.

Target behavior (what we want)
A) Redirect semantics that streaming clients can handle deterministically
- If a redirect is required before we start streaming, return a redirect response (307) with Location, and DO NOT begin streaming body.
- If a redirect decision happens mid-stream, do NOT attempt to redirect in-band. Instead, terminate with a clear, machine-actionable error response at the stream boundary (see below).

B) Idempotency + resumability guarantees
- For streaming Chat/Generate requests, if client supplies Idempotency-Key, the request should be deduped across retries and across regions (within a bounded TTL).
- Server returns stable identifiers:
  - x-redwood-request-id: stable across retries when idempotency is used
  - x-redwood-stream-id: stable stream identity used for resume
  - SSE event id: monotonic sequence for already-emitted chunks
- A reconnect attempt should be able to resume using Last-Event-ID (SSE) + stream-id (or request-id) without duplicating tokens.

C) Failover-safe termination and guidance
- When we must cut a stream due to failover, return a terminal SSE event:
  event: redwood.error
  data: {"code":"REGION_FAILOVER","retryable":true,"retry_after_ms":<n>,"resume":true}
  and then close.
- Document recommended client behavior (SDKs + docs): on REGION_FAILOVER, reconnect with the same Idempotency-Key and include Last-Event-ID.

Implementation plan
1) Gateway / edge behavior
- Ensure we never emit HTTP redirects after streaming has started (headers sent). If reroute is needed mid-stream, terminate stream with REGION_FAILOVER terminal event.
- For pre-stream redirects: use 307 (method-preserving), include Location and x-redwood-redirect-reason=FAILOVER.

2) Backend request identity
- Persist idempotency mapping for streaming requests with bounded TTL (default 15m; configurable) so retries in a new region resolve to the same request_id.
- Enforce that idempotency dedupe returns the existing stream (resume) rather than starting a new generation.

3) Resume support
- Support Last-Event-ID in streaming endpoints: if provided and matches stream-id/request-id, suppress re-sending prior chunks and continue.
- If resume is not possible (e.g., KV cache evicted / stream TTL expired), return a clear non-resumable error code that the client can treat as a fresh start.

4) Testing
- Unit tests: redirect-before-stream, terminate-mid-stream-with-error-event, idempotency mapping reuse, Last-Event-ID resume path.
- Integration tests in staging using controlled policy flips (us-east -> eu-west) during active streams.

Acceptance criteria
- During a forced us-east -> eu-west failover in staging, >= 95% of streaming requests either:
  - continue without client-visible interruption, OR
  - terminate with REGION_FAILOVER and successfully resume without duplicated output when retried with Idempotency-Key + Last-Event-ID.
- No duplicate token emission for a single logical request when idempotency is present (validated via request_id + token accounting).
- No increase in gateway error budget burn attributable to redirect loops.

Rollout
- Feature-flagged at gateway + API service: streaming_failover_resume_v1
- Staging: enable for internal tenants + canary routes for 48h
- Production: 5% Hosted traffic (non-enterprise) -> 25% -> 100% contingent on:
  - streaming_disconnect_rate
  - duplicate_request_rate (idempotency collisions vs expected)
  - customer-reported regressions

Links / references
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-19821-streaming-reconnect-on-redirect.json
- Related gateway work: ENG-2419 (timeouts/retries/circuit breakers)
- Support ticket: sources/jira/customer-support/SUP-25322-streaming-disconnects-during-region-redirect.json
- Game day context: controlled failover 2026-01-15 (see game day results + slack incident thread)

Notes
This ticket is focused on server/gateway behavior + platform semantics. SDK-specific improvements and docs updates will be tracked separately if needed, but any behavior changes here should be compatible with existing clients (best-effort) and provide clearer machine-actionable failure modes.

title: Post-game-day action items: regional failover (us-east → eu-west) hardening
preview:
Tracking ticket for issues found during Game Day 1 (controlled production failover: us-east → eu-west) and the immediate hardening work required before expanding automation to additional routes/models/tiers.

Background
- Test window: 2026-01-15 (see: Controlled Failover Test Results 2026-01-15; #incidents thread "controlled-failover-us-east-to-eu-west-live")
- Scope exercised: Hosted API + limited Dedicated tenants behind feature flags; dry-run + execute paths; rollback/hold-down timers; capacity gating; DNS TTL changes already applied.
- Outcome summary:
  - Automation executed the planned routing shift within target, but we observed (a) transient 5xx/timeouts for a subset of traffic, (b) elevated tail latency in eu-west immediately after shift, and (c) confusion on dashboards due to missing/late annotations.
  - One rollback was triggered sooner than expected due to hold-down/anti-flap semantics + a noisy latency burn signal.

Success criteria we missed / partially met
- Failover time: met for primary routes; borderline for a few long-lived streaming sessions.
- Customer-visible errors: not fully met (transient 5xx/timeouts observed; Support received at least one inbound).
- Operator experience: not met (dashboard interpretation/annotations; runbook gaps; decision evidence not obvious in the moment).

Action items (prioritized)
P0 (must land before expanding automation beyond current blast radius)
1) Add automation-driven dashboard annotations + incident timeline markers
   - Problem: during the test, graphs were hard to interpret without clear “failover started / policy flipped / drain started / rollback” markers.
   - Owner: Sergio Costa (dashboards) + Devon Price (incident-bot)
   - Deliverable: annotations emitted from controller audit events; verify Grafana/observability-pack bundle updates.
   - Links: INT-1149 (dashboards missing failover annotations), incident-bot PR-412, redwood PR-19844.

2) Tune gateway retry budgets + standardize Retry-After behavior during failover
   - Problem: client retries amplified load during redirect window; some 5xx bursts likely due to thundering herd + uneven backoff.
   - Owner: Peter Holtz
   - Deliverable: finalized retry/retry-after semantics + updated defaults in gateway; validate with synthetic load and staged failover.
   - Links: redwood PR-19820, redwood PR-19827, runtime thread on graceful degradation.

3) Fix / validate streaming reconnect behavior on region redirect for long-lived sessions
   - Problem: streaming sessions showed higher disconnect rate; reconnect wasn’t consistently idempotent under redirect.
   - Owner: Vikram Singh
   - Deliverable: client-visible behavior documented + server-side redirect handling; add regression tests.
   - Links: ENG-2422, redwood PR-19821, SUP-25322.

4) Anti-flap / rollback semantics: adjust hold-down + corroboration rules for latency burn
   - Problem: rollback triggered earlier than intended due to latency burn signal noise; operators unsure whether rollback was “correct” or conservative.
   - Owner: Ravi Menon + Jordan Lee
   - Deliverable: updated thresholds + required corroboration for latency-only impairment; document reasoning in config and runbook.
   - Links: ENG-2416, ADR-043, #architecture thread "adr-043-rollback-guardrails".

5) Capacity gating: prevent failover into insufficient headroom; clarify headroom computation
   - Problem: eu-west tail latency spiked after shift; capacity signal existed but wasn’t strict enough / not clearly visible to on-call.
   - Owner: Vanessa Ortiz
   - Deliverable: revise “safe-to-failover” gate to account for (a) warm KV cache impact, (b) surge margin for retries, (c) Dedicated reservations.
   - Links: ENG-2414, redwood PR-19823, SUP-25341, capacity sheet "region-capacity-and-drain-plan-us-east-eu-west".

6) Controller robustness: missing-signal safety + clearer evidence bundle in dry-run output
   - Problem: rehearsal/staging previously hit missing-signal crash; in prod, missing or delayed signals still created ambiguity.
   - Owner: Miles Thornton + Benji Okafor
   - Deliverable: enforce “unknown signal” behavior + evidence summary (top signals, confidence, timers) in CLI + incident-bot summaries.
   - Links: redwood PR-19840, ENG-2415, #eng-sre thread "dry-run-mode-acceptance-criteria".

P1 (should land before next production game day)
7) Metric label/cardinality review follow-through + SLO-friendly aggregations
   - Problem: some panels/alerts were noisy or expensive; reason/source dimensions risked cardinality spikes.
   - Owner: Allison Grant + Aisha Bello
   - Deliverable: finalize label set (bounded enums), publish dashboard spec updates, and confirm burn alerts map to regional impairment definition.
   - Links: ENG-2418, redwood PR-19816, redwood PR-19829, "failover-metrics-cardinality-review".

8) Add “decision audit trail” retention + export guidance for enterprise requests
   - Problem: enterprise customers asked for audit evidence of failover actions; ensure retention is clear and retrieval is easy.
   - Owner: Ethan Park
   - Deliverable: document retention + retrieval path; ensure audit events are linkable to incident timeline and dashboards.
   - Links: redwood PR-19824, SUP-25444.

9) Dedicated drain sequencing: tighten safeguards + add progress metrics to default dashboards
   - Problem: drain workflow worked but progress wasn’t visible enough; need clearer abort conditions and owner handoffs.
   - Owner: Irene Choi + Keiko Yamamoto
   - Deliverable: progress metrics + runbook updates; ensure drain does not begin until routing policy flip is confirmed and capacity gate passes.
   - Links: ENG-2420, redwood PR-19822, redwood PR-19842, GPU pool drain runbook.

10) Cross-region reachability probes: validate signal freshness + align thresholds with controller timers
   - Problem: reachability signal was helpful but we need tighter alignment between probe intervals, TTLs, and hold-down timers.
   - Owner: Victor Alvarez + Emily Zhao
   - Deliverable: confirm SLO for probe freshness; add alert when probe data is stale; tune to avoid false positives.
   - Links: ENG-2417, perf-canary PR-188, networking notes on DNS/anycast.

P2 (nice-to-have before wider rollout; can proceed in parallel)
11) Runbook refresh: make ownership boundaries explicit (Routing vs Infra vs SRE), add “how to tell it’s real” section
   - Problem: operators lost time on “is this a real region impairment or local service issue?” and “who owns what step?”
   - Owner: Tomáš Novák + Kira Thompson
   - Deliverable: update Hosted API regional failover runbook + controller runbook; include quick triage checklist and links to the exact dashboards.
   - Links: ENG-2423, #eng-oncall thread "runbook-gaps-found-in-rehearsal".

12) Comms: pre-written customer response snippets for planned tests vs real incidents
   - Problem: Support needed quick, consistent language when customers noticed transient errors.
   - Owner: Dev Patel
   - Deliverable: merge comms playbook snippet into internal Support KB; align with status page template.
   - Links: SUP-25310, comms playbook + status page template.

Acceptance criteria (for closing this ticket)
- All P0 items are complete and validated in staging via replay/simulation or a controlled rehearsal.
- Next production game day has a documented “go/no-go” checklist that includes:
  - dashboard annotations verified
  - retry-after enabled and observed in metrics
  - streaming reconnect regression tests passing
  - capacity gate thresholds reviewed with Fleet
  - rollback semantics documented and explained to on-call
- Post-change: paging noise reduced vs baseline while maintaining fast detection of true region impairment.

Notes / links
- Related umbrella: ENG-2411 (regional failover automation upgrade)
- ADRs: ADR-042 (signal hierarchy), ADR-043 (rollback)
- Key PRs referenced above: region health aggregator, policy engine priority evaluation, dry-run mode, rollback safety, SLO metrics, burn alerts, gateway timeout tuning, streaming reconnect, audit log, capacity signal integration.

Next update
- Rafael to post weekly status in #eng-sre with a checklist view of P0/P1 completion and any new findings from staging rehearsals.

title: Instrument routing fallback_reason enums and map to incident taxonomy
preview:
Problem
Routing-related incidents have been repeatedly misclassified during live response ("routing bug" vs "fleet capacity" vs "regional health"), which slows escalation and weakens reporting. Today, router emits a mix of freeform logs and opaque counters (e.g., "fallback=true") without a single, low-cardinality explanation.

This issue adds a structured `fallback_reason` signal to router decisions (and downstream logs/metrics/traces) with a strict enum allowlist. We also define a deterministic mapping from `fallback_reason` (+ a small number of qualifiers) to the v1 incident taxonomy buckets so incident-bot and on-call can quickly classify and route to the correct primary owner.

Goals
- Add `fallback_reason` as a low-cardinality enum emitted on every request that uses a fallback path (model variant, region change, or pool change).
- Ensure we can distinguish at least: capacity, health, policy, timeout.
- Emit signals in a way that is safe for metrics cardinality (allowlist only, no freeform reasons).
- Provide a canonical mapping from fallback_reason -> incident taxonomy type/subtype for live classification and reporting.
- Make it easy to correlate fallback spikes with customer-visible symptoms (5xx, 429, p95 latency, streaming disconnects).

Non-goals
- This does not decide who owns the incident taxonomy overall (see ENG-4821 / ENG-4825).
- This does not implement paging/routing changes in PagerDuty/alert routing (see ENG-4823).
- This does not attempt to infer root cause; it only records the router’s decision basis.

Proposed `fallback_reason` enum (v1)
- `capacity`: router could not select the preferred target because capacity was unavailable or below policy thresholds.
  - examples: queue depth over limit, no allocatable slots, GPU saturation guardrail triggered
- `health`: preferred target(s) excluded due to health signal(s) indicating unavailability/degradation.
  - examples: endpoint marked unhealthy, consecutive 5xx, health check failing
- `policy`: a routing policy intentionally steered traffic away from the preferred target.
  - examples: customer pinning to a region/model, allow/deny lists, compliance constraints, canary/rollout policy
- `timeout`: router could not complete selection/verification within the allowed time.
  - examples: backend selection RPC timeout, service discovery timeout, health read timeout

Notes
- No `unknown` in v1 for metrics. If we cannot confidently assign one of the above, we must:
  1) emit a structured error log `fallback_reason_resolution_failed=true`, and
  2) default to `timeout` if the operation exceeded a deadline, else `health` if the target was excluded by health gating, else `capacity` if capacity gating triggered. The goal is to keep dashboards usable and force engineering to tighten instrumentation.

Where this is emitted
1) Request logs
- Add `router.fallback_applied` (boolean)
- Add `router.fallback_reason` (enum above)
- Add `router.fallback_from` and `router.fallback_to` as normalized identifiers (NOT raw strings):
  - from/to pool_id, region_id, model_id
  - avoid embedding customer IDs or dynamic shard IDs

2) Metrics
- Counter: `router_fallback_total{reason=<enum>, tier=<hosted|dedicated|private>}`
- Counter: `router_request_total{tier=...}` (existing; use to compute rate)
- Optional gauge/histogram already exists for selection latency; add `reason` label ONLY on the fallback counter to avoid cardinality blowups.

3) Tracing
- Add span attribute `fallback_reason` to router selection span.
- Add span event when fallback is applied: `router_fallback_applied` with reason and from/to normalized IDs.

4) Alert payloads (follow-up)
- This ticket only guarantees the reason exists in logs/metrics/traces.
- ENG-4823 can plumb `fallback_reason` into alert payloads and incident-bot suggestions.

Mapping to incident taxonomy (v1)
This mapping is for primary incident_type selection when the dominant symptom is "unexpected fallback" or "fallback spike".

- `capacity` -> Incident Type: Capacity/Fleet Shortage
  - Subtype guidance:
    - if regional: "Regional capacity shortfall"
    - if dedicated pool only: "Dedicated pool saturation"
  - Primary owner: Infra/Fleet
  - Common co-factors to record (contributing systems): Routing, Control Plane

- `health` -> Incident Type: Availability Degradation (5xx/overload)
  - Subtype guidance:
    - if health is driven by backend 5xx: "Serving backend errors"
    - if health is driven by regional health monitors: "Region degradation"
  - Primary owner: Serving Runtime if backend errors; Infra/Fleet if region degradation signal is due to node failures; SRE to arbitrate in Sev0 until confirmed
  - Note: if the router health signal itself is wrong (false positives), classify as Routing/Fallback Misbehavior

- `policy` -> Incident Type: Routing/Fallback Misbehavior
  - Subtype guidance:
    - "Policy/config regression" if a recent config change or rollout is implicated
    - "Incorrect constraint evaluation" if policies are correct but router evaluates incorrectly
  - Primary owner: Platform Control Plane + Smart Routing
  - Escalate to Product/CS only if customer-config changes were involved and we need coordination

- `timeout` -> Incident Type: Control Plane Outage OR Private Connectivity/Config (context-dependent)
  - Hosted/Dedicated default: Control Plane Outage (routing dependency timeout)
  - Private default: Private deployment connectivity/config issues (if timeouts are from customer network path)
  - Primary owner: Platform Control Plane for hosted/dedicated; Private Deployments/Networking for private; consult SRE

Edge-case rules (documented for on-call)
- If fallback_reason is `capacity` and 429s are the dominant symptom in Dedicated, keep primary as Capacity/Fleet Shortage (not Quota), unless there is explicit evidence of quota enforcement.
- If fallback_reason is `policy` and the change was customer-initiated (customer updated routing policy), classify as Routing/Policy Misconfiguration (still owned by Routing, but CS comms differs).
- If multiple reasons occur simultaneously, pick the highest-volume reason as primary. Record the others as contributing factors in the incident record/postmortem.

Acceptance criteria
- [x] Router emits `fallback_reason` for all fallback paths (model fallback, region fallback, pool fallback).
- [x] Enum is documented and stable (allowlist), with unit tests for mapping logic.
- [x] Counter `router_fallback_total{reason,tier}` exists and is validated in staging + one prod canary region.
- [x] No new unbounded labels added; Observability sign-off on cardinality risk.
- [x] Confluence note added to taxonomy doc linking fallback_reason -> incident type guidance (SRE will incorporate).

Implementation plan
- Add a small "reason resolution" module in router:
  - collect the first gating condition that excluded the preferred target (capacity, health, policy)
  - if selection fails due to deadline, force `timeout`
- Ensure reason is computed once and passed through to:
  - logs
  - metrics counter increment
  - trace attribute
- Add a golden test suite:
  - capacity gate triggers -> capacity
  - health gate triggers -> health
  - policy routing override -> policy
  - discovery RPC exceeds deadline -> timeout
- Rollout:
  - behind flag `router_fallback_reason_v1`
  - enable in staging for 24h
  - enable in 1 hosted region (canary) for 24h
  - enable globally

Risks / open questions
- Some "capacity" conditions look like "timeout" when upstream signals lag (e.g., allocator RPC times out but real cause is capacity). We bias to `timeout` when deadlines hit for deterministic classification; postmortems can refine contributing factors.
- Private tier timeouts are ambiguous (customer network vs our control plane). We’ll rely on tier + dependency name to classify defaults; SRE to refine in v1.1 based on backfill.

Owners / reviewers
- DRI: Mina Haddad (Smart Routing)
- Reviews: Allison Grant (Observability standards), Aisha Bello (dashboards/SLO), Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet), Ethan Park (Control Plane)

Links
- GitHub PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-28437-instrument-router-fallback-reasons-for-taxonomy.json
- Related tickets: ENG-4821 (taxonomy definitions), ENG-4823 (alert payload + paging), ENG-4826 (metric label guardrails)
- Incident threads with misclassification examples: #incidents 1762210207, 1763194772
text:
Problem
Routing-related incidents have been repeatedly misclassified during live response ("routing bug" vs "fleet capacity" vs "regional health"), which slows escalation and weakens reporting. Today, router emits a mix of freeform logs and opaque counters (e.g., "fallback=true") without a single, low-cardinality explanation.

This issue adds a structured `fallback_reason` signal to router decisions (and downstream logs/metrics/traces) with a strict enum allowlist. We also define a deterministic mapping from `fallback_reason` (+ a small number of qualifiers) to the v1 incident taxonomy buckets so incident-bot and on-call can quickly classify and route to the correct primary owner.

Goals
- Add `fallback_reason` as a low-cardinality enum emitted on every request that uses a fallback path (model variant, region change, or pool change).
- Ensure we can distinguish at least: capacity, health, policy, timeout.
- Emit signals in a way that is safe for metrics cardinality (allowlist only, no freeform reasons).
- Provide a canonical mapping from fallback_reason -> incident taxonomy type/subtype for live classification and reporting.
- Make it easy to correlate fallback spikes with customer-visible symptoms (5xx, 429, p95 latency, streaming disconnects).

Non-goals
- This does not decide who owns the incident taxonomy overall (see ENG-4821 / ENG-4825).
- This does not implement paging/routing changes in PagerDuty/alert routing (see ENG-4823).
- This does not attempt to infer root cause; it only records the router’s decision basis.

- Add `router.fallback_applied` (boolean)
- Add `router.fallback_reason` (enum above)
- Add `router.fallback_from` and `router.fallback_to` as normalized identifiers (NOT raw strings):
  - from/to pool_id, region_id, model_id
  - avoid embedding customer IDs or dynamic shard IDs

2) Metrics

- Counter: `router_request_total{tier=...}` (existing; use to compute rate)

3) Tracing
- Add span attribute `fallback_reason` to router selection span.
- Add span event when fallback is applied: `router_fallback_applied` with reason and from/to normalized IDs.

4) Alert payloads (follow-up)
- This ticket only guarantees the reason exists in logs/metrics/traces.
- ENG-4823 can plumb `fallback_reason` into alert payloads and incident-bot suggestions.

Mapping to incident taxonomy (v1)
This mapping is for primary incident_type selection when the dominant symptom is "unexpected fallback" or "fallback spike".

- `capacity` -> Incident Type: Capacity/Fleet Shortage
  - Subtype guidance:
    - if regional: "Regional capacity shortfall"
    - if dedicated pool only: "Dedicated pool saturation"
  - Primary owner: Infra/Fleet
  - Common co-factors to record (contributing systems): Routing, Control Plane

- `health` -> Incident Type: Availability Degradation (5xx/overload)
  - Subtype guidance:
    - if health is driven by backend 5xx: "Serving backend errors"
    - if health is driven by regional health monitors: "Region degradation"
  - Primary owner: Serving Runtime if backend errors; Infra/Fleet if region degradation signal is due to node failures; SRE to arbitrate in Sev0 until confirmed
  - Note: if the router health signal itself is wrong (false positives), classify as Routing/Fallback Misbehavior

- `policy` -> Incident Type: Routing/Fallback Misbehavior
  - Subtype guidance:
    - "Policy/config regression" if a recent config change or rollout is implicated
    - "Incorrect constraint evaluation" if policies are correct but router evaluates incorrectly
  - Primary owner: Platform Control Plane + Smart Routing
  - Escalate to Product/CS only if customer-config changes were involved and we need coordination

- `timeout` -> Incident Type: Control Plane Outage OR Private Connectivity/Config (context-dependent)
  - Hosted/Dedicated default: Control Plane Outage (routing dependency timeout)
  - Private default: Private deployment connectivity/config issues (if timeouts are from customer network path)
  - Primary owner: Platform Control Plane for hosted/dedicated; Private Deployments/Networking for private; consult SRE

title: Game Day 1: controlled failover us-east → eu-west (Hosted API + Dedicated)
preview:
## Summary
Execute the first controlled production failover game day (us-east → eu-west) to validate end-to-end automation: trigger signal pipeline, deterministic policy evaluation, guardrails/rollback, circuit breakers/load shedding, observability, and comms.

This is an operational ticket for run-of-show, checklists, comms plan, and sign-offs.

## Scope (this game day)
- **Hosted API**: a limited set of routes/models behind feature flags; observe client behavior, streaming reconnect, gateway retries.
- **Dedicated**: a small set of opted-in test tenants/pools; validate drain/evacuation workflow gates and progress metrics.
- **Failover controller**: start in dry-run, then execute approved policy flips.

Out of scope: Private deployments (only validate documentation messaging if asked).

## Links / Artifacts
- Confluence plan: `sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/controlled-failover-test-plan-q1-2026.json`
- Comms playbook: `sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/incident-process/regional-failover-communication-playbook.json`
- Status page template: `sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comms/status-page-template-regional-event.json`
- Routing controller runbook: `sources/confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/routing-policy-failover-controller-runbook.json`
- Hosted API failover runbook: `sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/hosted-api-regional-failover-runbook.json`
- Dedicated drain procedure: `sources/confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/gpu-pool-drain-and-evacuation-procedure.json`
- Dashboard spec: `sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/failover-signal-dashboards-spec.json`
- Drive checklist sheet: `sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/site-reliability-engineering/failover-test-checklist-sheet.json`
- Capacity plan: `sources/google_drive/shared_drives/engineering/infrastructure/region-capacity-and-drain-plan-us-east-eu-west.json`

## Owners / Roles (day-of)
- Incident commander (IC): **Rafael Mendes**
- Failover controller operator: **Miles Thornton**
- Routing/policy liaison: **Ravi Menon** / **Jordan Lee**
- Control plane liaison: **Ethan Park**
- Observability lead: **Allison Grant** (backup: Sergio Costa)
- Network reachability liaison: **Victor Alvarez**
- Dedicated drain liaison: **Irene Choi** (ops) + **Vanessa Ortiz** (capacity)
- Support/customer liaison: **Dev Patel** (backup: Owen Phillips)
- Product approver for customer messaging: **Nikhil Sharma** (Hosted) / **Lauren Bishop** (Dedicated)

## Proposed Schedule (PT)
- T-60m: go/no-go review, confirm headroom, verify dashboards, confirm rollback authority
- T-30m: enable dry-run in prod for scoped tenants/routes; verify audit events + incident-bot summaries
- T-10m: post internal heads-up in #eng-sre and #support; status page pre-update (planned test)
- T0: inject impairment / flip traffic steering per plan (policy flip)
- T+5m to T+30m: observe stabilization + burn-rate; validate Dedicated drain gates
- T+30m: begin rollback to normal routing (if not already auto-rolled back)
- T+45m: post “complete” update + monitoring period
- T+60m: closeout + capture metrics and follow-ups

## Pre-flight Checklist (must complete for go)
### Readiness prerequisites
- [ ] Feature flags set for limited blast radius (tenant allowlist + routes/models allowlist)
- [ ] Failover controller deployed with **dry-run** available and confirmed producing recommendations
- [ ] Approval hooks configured (require on-call ack for execution) for production
- [ ] Audit events are visible (who/what/why) and linked to dashboards
- [ ] Perf canary cross-region probes green (reachability + latency within thresholds)
- [ ] Capacity headroom confirmed in eu-west (per capacity plan): **>= 30% headroom** for scoped workload
- [ ] Gateway circuit breaker config confirmed for failover window (retry budget, timeouts, Retry-After behavior)
- [ ] Dedicated drain endpoint tested in staging for the targeted pools and produces progress metrics

### Observability / dashboards (must be on screen)
- [ ] Region health summary: reachability + error burn + latency burn + capacity
- [ ] Policy flips timeline (annotations enabled)
- [ ] API gateway 5xx, timeouts, and retry rates
- [ ] p50/p95/p99 latency by region + route
- [ ] Dedicated drain progress: queued requests, in-flight, evacuation rate, error counts

### Alerting / paging hygiene
- [ ] Confirm which alerts page vs ticket during test; document temporary routing for noise suppression
- [ ] Confirm incident-bot is enabled for failover event summaries

### Comms readiness
- [ ] Internal comms template ready (IC updates cadence)
- [ ] Support brief prepared (what customers may see; how to answer)
- [ ] Status page “planned test” copy prepared and scheduled
- [ ] Enterprise proactive notifications: list of accounts + contacts approved (Support + Product)

## Execution Steps (run-of-show)
1) **Go/No-Go (IC)**
   - Confirm cloud provider status is nominal; confirm no active incidents in us-east or eu-west.
   - Confirm eu-west headroom and autoscaling limits.
   - Confirm rollback authority: IC (Rafael) + executive escalation (Sean/Neha) on-call.

2) **Enable dry-run (Operator: Miles)**
   - Turn on dry-run for scoped tenants/routes.
   - Validate output includes: recommended actions, trigger evidence, confidence/corroboration signals.
   - Confirm audit log entries are emitted.

3) **Comms start (IC + Support liaison)**
   - Post internal update in #incidents (tag @oncall-sre, @routing, @infra, @support).
   - Post status page pre-update: “Planned failover test; brief elevated latency/errors possible for a limited subset.”

4) **Initiate failover policy flip (Operator: Miles; Approval: Rafael)**
   - Execute policy change: steer scoped traffic from us-east primary to eu-west.
   - Confirm the controller records: reason=planned_test, from=us-east, to=eu-west, scoped_entities.
   - Confirm policy engine evaluation is deterministic and matches expected hierarchy (reachability overrides, burn rates corroborated, hold-down timers applied).

5) **Validate system behavior (Allison + Ravi + Victor)**
   - Confirm reachability probes reflect impairment/redirect appropriately.
   - Watch for: increased retries, gateway overload, p99 spikes in eu-west; confirm load shedding triggers emit Retry-After.
   - Confirm no unscoped tenants/routes are impacted.

6) **Dedicated drain validation (Irene + Vanessa)**
   - If included in scenario: initiate drain for targeted Dedicated pool(s) and confirm safety gates.
   - Confirm drain progress metrics and that routing honors drain state.

7) **Rollback / return to steady state (Operator + IC)**
   - If success criteria met: roll back to normal policy after monitoring window.
   - If rollback triggers are hit: execute immediate rollback and/or disable automation.

8) **Closeout**
   - Post final internal + status page updates.
   - Capture key timestamps (T0, detection, flip executed, stabilization, rollback).
   - Create follow-up issues for any regressions.

## Success Criteria (must be measurable)
- Failover policy flip completes within **≤ 5 minutes** of execution approval.
- For scoped traffic during failover window:
  - 5xx rate increase remains **< 0.5% absolute** for >5 minutes.
  - p99 latency increase remains **< 2x baseline** for >10 minutes.
  - No sustained overload loops (retry storm) observed; Retry-After is emitted when shedding.
- No impact to unscoped tenants/routes (verified by dashboards + sampling logs).
- Automation decisions are audit-logged and visible to on-call within **≤ 2 minutes**.

## Rollback Triggers (stop the test)
- Scoped 5xx rate **> 1.0%** for 5 consecutive minutes.
- eu-west p99 latency **> 2.5x baseline** for 10 minutes.
- Capacity headroom in eu-west drops below **15%** (or autoscaling hits hard limit).
- Control plane errors cause inability to modify/verify policy state.
- Unexpected blast radius (any unscoped tenant impact confirmed).

## Comms Plan
### Internal
- Channel: #incidents (primary), #eng-sre, #eng-platform, #eng-infra, #support
- Cadence: every 10–15 minutes during active phase; announce start, flip executed, stabilization, rollback, end.

### External
- Status page: planned test template with clear “limited subset” language + timestamps.
- Support macro: explain planned reliability test, potential transient errors/streaming reconnect, recommend retry with backoff.
- Proactive outreach: only to enterprise accounts identified by Support + Product; no broad email.

## Required Sign-offs (go/no-go)
- SRE leadership: **Sean Gallagher** (or delegate)
- Exec sponsor for production test: **Neha Kapoor**
- Routing readiness: **Mina Haddad**
- Control plane readiness: **Ethan Park**
- Networking/DNS readiness: **Irene Choi** + **Victor Alvarez**
- Observability readiness: **Allison Grant**
- Support readiness: **Dev Patel**

## Post-game-day deliverables
- Link results doc + timeline + metrics snapshot
- Action items ticket(s) with owners/dates
- Update runbooks based on gaps observed

---
### Notes
- Use `planned_test` reason code for any automation actions so alert annotations and audit searches are clean.
- Keep Dedicated scope minimal; prioritize Hosted API signal quality + routing correctness for Game Day 1.

## Execution Steps (run-of-show)
1) **Go/No-Go (IC)**
   - Confirm cloud provider status is nominal; confirm no active incidents in us-east or eu-west.
   - Confirm eu-west headroom and autoscaling limits.
   - Confirm rollback authority: IC (Rafael) + executive escalation (Sean/Neha) on-call.

2) **Enable dry-run (Operator: Miles)**
   - Turn on dry-run for scoped tenants/routes.
   - Validate output includes: recommended actions, trigger evidence, confidence/corroboration signals.
   - Confirm audit log entries are emitted.

3) **Comms start (IC + Support liaison)**
   - Post internal update in #incidents (tag @oncall-sre, @routing, @infra, @support).
   - Post status page pre-update: “Planned failover test; brief elevated latency/errors possible for a limited subset.”

4) **Initiate failover policy flip (Operator: Miles; Approval: Rafael)**
   - Execute policy change: steer scoped traffic from us-east primary to eu-west.
   - Confirm the controller records: reason=planned_test, from=us-east, to=eu-west, scoped_entities.
   - Confirm policy engine evaluation is deterministic and matches expected hierarchy (reachability overrides, burn rates corroborated, hold-down timers applied).

5) **Validate system behavior (Allison + Ravi + Victor)**
   - Confirm reachability probes reflect impairment/redirect appropriately.
   - Watch for: increased retries, gateway overload, p99 spikes in eu-west; confirm load shedding triggers emit Retry-After.
   - Confirm no unscoped tenants/routes are impacted.

6) **Dedicated drain validation (Irene + Vanessa)**
   - If included in scenario: initiate drain for targeted Dedicated pool(s) and confirm safety gates.
   - Confirm drain progress metrics and that routing honors drain state.

7) **Rollback / return to steady state (Operator + IC)**
   - If success criteria met: roll back to normal policy after monitoring window.
   - If rollback triggers are hit: execute immediate rollback and/or disable automation.

8) **Closeout**
   - Post final internal + status page updates.
   - Capture key timestamps (T0, detection, flip executed, stabilization, rollback).
   - Create follow-up issues for any regressions.

## Success Criteria (must be measurable)
- Failover policy flip completes within **≤ 5 minutes** of execution approval.
- For scoped traffic during failover window:
  - 5xx rate increase remains **< 0.5% absolute** for >5 minutes.
  - p99 latency increase remains **< 2x baseline** for >10 minutes.
  - No sustained overload loops (retry storm) observed; Retry-After is emitted when shedding.
- No impact to unscoped tenants/routes (verified by dashboards + sampling logs).
- Automation decisions are audit-logged and visible to on-call within **≤ 2 minutes**.

## Rollback Triggers (stop the test)
- Scoped 5xx rate **> 1.0%** for 5 consecutive minutes.
- eu-west p99 latency **> 2.5x baseline** for 10 minutes.
- Capacity headroom in eu-west drops below **15%** (or autoscaling hits hard limit).
- Control plane errors cause inability to modify/verify policy state.
- Unexpected blast radius (any unscoped tenant impact confirmed).

## Comms Plan
### Internal
- Channel: #incidents (primary), #eng-sre, #eng-platform, #eng-infra, #support
- Cadence: every 10–15 minutes during active phase; announce start, flip executed, stabilization, rollback, end.

### External
- Status page: planned test template with clear “limited subset” language + timestamps.
- Support macro: explain planned reliability test, potential transient errors/streaming reconnect, recommend retry with backoff.
- Proactive outreach: only to enterprise accounts identified by Support + Product; no broad email.

## Required Sign-offs (go/no-go)
- SRE leadership: **Sean Gallagher** (or delegate)
- Exec sponsor for production test: **Neha Kapoor**
- Routing readiness: **Mina Haddad**
- Control plane readiness: **Ethan Park**
- Networking/DNS readiness: **Irene Choi** + **Victor Alvarez**
- Observability readiness: **Allison Grant**
- Support readiness: **Dev Patel**

## Post-game-day deliverables
- Link results doc + timeline + metrics snapshot
- Action items ticket(s) with owners/dates
- Update runbooks based on gaps observed

---
### Notes
- Use `planned_test` reason code for any automation actions so alert annotations and audit searches are clean.
- Keep Dedicated scope minimal; prioritize Hosted API signal quality + routing correctness for Game Day 1.

title: Add failover metrics + multi-region SLO burn-rate alerts for regional impairment detection
preview:
Context / Problem
Regional failover automation work (trigger hierarchy, controller, rollback) needs first-class observability so on-call can:
1) detect true regional impairment quickly (without paging on local/service-specific noise)
2) understand *why* the system is failing over (reachability vs 5xx burn vs latency burn vs capacity)
3) verify the outcome (did we reduce impact and stay within error budgets?)

Today we have partial metrics (gateway 5xx, some latency dashboards) but no standardized event metrics for failover decisions, and burn-rate alerts are inconsistent across Hosted API vs Dedicated routes/tiers. We also had staging rehearsal feedback that alerts were noisy and dashboards lacked automation annotations.

Goal
Create a minimal-but-sufficient set of metrics and burn-rate alerts that:
- power the failover signal dashboards and game day success criteria
- reduce time-to-detect a true region-level event
- capture the trigger hierarchy outcome (what signal(s) crossed threshold and what action was taken)
- remain cardinality-safe (avoid per-tenant / per-model label explosions)

Scope
In-scope:
- new metrics for failover events + trigger reasons (recommended, executed, rollback, suppressed)
- multi-window burn-rate alerts for multi-region availability SLOs (Hosted + Dedicated)
- alert annotations/runbook links, and guidance on paging routing
- dashboard annotations for key events (policy flips, controller decisions)

Out of scope (for this ticket):
- changing failover controller behavior (see ENG-2415/2416)
- customer-facing SLO reporting/UX (PM-513)

Proposed metrics
1) Failover event counters (low cardinality)
- Metric: failover_events_total
- Type: counter
- Labels (MUST remain bounded):
  - environment: prod|staging
  - product: hosted_api|dedicated
  - event_type: recommendation|execution|rollback|suppressed|dry_run
  - reason: reachability|error_burn|latency_burn|capacity|operator_initiated|unknown
  - source: controller|policy_engine|operator
  - from_region: <bounded set>
  - to_region: <bounded set>
  - tenant_tier: free|pro|enterprise (no tenant_id)

2) Failover decision timing
- Metric: failover_decision_latency_seconds
- Type: histogram
- Meaning: time from first corroborated impairment signal to decision emission (recommendation/execution)
- Same bounded labels as above, but avoid from_region/to_region if it causes blowups; keep only product/event_type/reason/env.

3) Region impairment signal snapshots (aggregated)
- Metric family: region_impairment_signal
- Gauge(s) exported by the region health aggregator/policy engine evaluation path:
  - region_reachability_ok (0/1)
  - region_error_burn_rate (float)
  - region_latency_burn_rate (float)
  - region_capacity_headroom (float)
- Labels: environment, product, region

4) Audit event -> metrics bridge (optional)
If the audit log is the source of truth (PR-19824), we can derive failover_events_total from structured audit events to ensure we count *what actually happened* vs what was evaluated.

Cardinality constraints / guardrails
- Do NOT label on: tenant_id, model, route, org_id, policy_name.
- Restrict reason enum to ADR-042 canonical set.
- If we need per-route drilldown, do it in logs/traces, not metrics.
- Add a lint/check in observability-pack rules bundle to prevent new labels from being added without review (follow-up if needed).

SLO burn-rate alerting
We want regional impairment detection that is:
- fast when the burn is severe (page quickly)
- stable under brief spikes (avoid false positives)

Burn-rate strategy (standard multi-window):
- Page (critical): burn_rate > 14x over 5m AND burn_rate > 7x over 30m
- Page (high): burn_rate > 6x over 30m AND burn_rate > 3x over 2h
- Ticket (warning): burn_rate > 1x over 6h

Notes:
- Exact thresholds should match the 2026 multi-region availability SLO spec (Hosted vs Dedicated may differ). If Dedicated has stricter latency SLOs but different availability objective, keep alerts SLO-specific.
- Alerts should key on “region-level event” aggregations (multi-region availability) to avoid paging for a single POP/node issue.

Alert routing + annotations
- Route critical pages to eng-oncall + eng-sre during game day window; post-game-day, default to eng-oncall with eng-sre secondary.
- Alert annotations must include:
  - link to failover signal dashboard
  - link to runbook entry point (Hosted API regional failover runbook + controller runbook)
  - a short hint to check corroboration: reachability probe panel + 5xx burn + capacity

Dashboards
This ticket is not the full dashboard spec, but we need enough to validate alerts:
- Panels:
  - failover_events_total by reason/event_type
  - region_reachability_ok by region
  - error burn + latency burn (regional)
  - capacity headroom gate
  - policy flips count (with automation annotations)
- Add annotations driven by automation (audit events) so the timeline is readable during incidents and game days.

Implementation plan
1) Add metrics in codebase
- Emit failover_events_total from controller + policy engine decision output.
- Ensure audit events (if present) include reason/source consistent with labels.

2) Add alert rules bundle
- Create burn-rate alert rules for:
  - hosted_api multi-region availability SLO
  - dedicated multi-region availability SLO
- Include alert labels: severity, product, environment, region (if appropriate), paging_team

3) Tuning + rehearsal
- Validate in staging using recorded impairment scenarios and game day rehearsal config.
- Confirm we do not exceed metric label cardinality budgets (use existing cardinality review checklist).

4) Rollout
- Ship alerts disabled by default in prod; enable in a controlled window with on-call present.
- During planned failover test, verify:
  - alerts trigger within target detection window
  - no duplicate pages for the same event
  - annotations show up on dashboards

Acceptance criteria
- Metrics:
  - failover_events_total emitted in staging and prod (dry-run at minimum)
  - reason/source/event_type present and matches ADR-042 hierarchy
  - no tenant/model/route labels
- Alerts:
  - burn-rate alert rules exist for Hosted + Dedicated multi-region SLOs
  - alert annotations include dashboard + runbook links
  - alert noise acceptable in staging rehearsal (<= 1 false positive per rehearsal scenario)
- Docs/Runbook:
  - brief on-call note added to runbook: how to interpret burn-rate alerts and which corroboration panels to check

Links / Related
- GitHub (in flight):
  - redwood PR-19816 add multi-region SLO metrics
  - redwood PR-19818 burn-rate alert rules
  - redwood PR-19829 reduce metrics cardinality for failover reasons
  - observability-pack PR-907 dashboards + alert rule bundles
  - incident-bot PR-412 failover event summaries (for context; not required but helpful)
- Related Linear:
  - ENG-2412 Define failover trigger signal hierarchy
  - ENG-2413 Implement region health aggregator service
  - ENG-2415 Dry-run mode for failover controller
  - ENG-2416 Automated routing policy rollback

Open questions
- Should burn-rate alerts page on-call directly, or page SRE first during initial rollout? Proposal: page on-call + SRE secondary for game day, then revisit after 2 weeks of signal quality.
- Do we want separate alerts for reachability (synthetic probes) vs SLO burn? Proposal: reachability alerts should be warning/ticket unless corroborated by SLO burn to avoid provider blips paging.

Risks
- Alert noise if we page on single-signal failures (reachability only). Mitigation: require corroboration or keep reachability as non-paging.
- Cardinality blow-ups if reason/source values are not constrained. Mitigation: enforce enums + code review + follow-up lint.

Owner notes
Allison: please coordinate with Sergio Costa for the alert rule bundle formatting and with Miles/Ravi on the canonical reason enum values so labels stay stable across services.

title: Incident-bot: notify #incidents when overload protection activates (shed/admission control)
preview:
Add structured incident-bot notifications when hot-route capacity protection activates so on-call can quickly distinguish expected best-effort shedding from enterprise-impacting overload events.

We need notifications for:
- sustained admission control (best-effort gated) on protected routes
- shed-rate threshold exceeded (active dropping) on protected routes

Primary destination: #incidents (and optionally #eng-oncall as a lower-noise mirror for early canary). Notifications must be deduped, rate-limited, and include links to the relevant dashboard + runbook section.
