title: Status page metrics backfill job (historical component health for incident summaries + trends)
preview:
## Context
We’re improving status page transparency by moving to a more granular component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability) and publishing higher-quality post-incident summaries quickly.

A recurring gap: we can draft incident summaries from the incident timeline, but we don’t have reliable historical component health rollups (error rate + latency) for the impacted components over the incident window and for pre/post comparisons. This is slowing Comms Leads down and causing “hand-wavy” impact statements.

This ticket adds a backfill job that populates historical component health metrics so:
- post-incident summaries can include concrete impact metrics (e.g., peak error rate, p95 latency, duration)
- we can trend component health over time and audit status accuracy

## Goals
1) Backfill component-level health rollups for a defined history window (initial proposal: 90 days; stretch: 180 days) for all public status components and regions.
2) Produce a dataset that is stable and queryable by:
   - incident summary generator (ENG-4823)
   - console linking / incident details view (Console integration spec)
   - weekly status accuracy audit job (pr-19278)
3) Ensure runs are idempotent and safe to rerun (late-arriving metrics, label fixes, re-computes).

## Non-goals
- Recomputing internal SLO burn-rate history (that lives in the SLO toolkit). This is a rollup for customer-facing reporting, not SLO accounting.
- Real-time status signal computation (covered by observability-pack anti-flap logic + alerts).

## Proposed outputs (rollups)
Rollups keyed by: component_id, region, capability (where applicable), time_bucket.

Time buckets:
- 1m or 5m rollups for incident windows (needed for peak calculations)
- 1h rollups for general querying
- 1d rollups for long-term trends (cheaper storage)

Metrics (minimum viable):
- request_count
- error_count + error_rate
- latency_p50 / latency_p95 (or p90+p99 depending on what’s stable in Mimir)
- streaming_disconnect_rate (streaming capability only)
- “data_quality_flags” (string field) when we detect missing labels / sparse data

## Data sources
Primary: Mimir/Prometheus metrics that already carry component tags (or can be mapped):
- API gateway metrics w/ component tags (see PR: redwood/pr-19502)
- Region-aware streaming error/disconnect metrics (see PR: redwood/pr-19518)
- Component registry source-of-truth (ADR-014; schema in observability-pack/pr-19371)

Secondary (fallback): service-level metrics mapped via the component mapping layer (ENG-4830) when direct component tags are absent.

## Where the data lives
Write to BigQuery dataset used by Observability + incident tooling (current preference from Allison/Aisha):
- table: status_component_health_rollups
- partition: date(time_bucket)
- clustering: component_id, region, capability

If BigQuery is blocked for cost/latency, alternate is ClickHouse (but that complicates integrations). Assume BigQuery for v1.

## Backfill approach
- Implement as a scheduled batch job (Airflow/Prefect—use whatever we’re already running for observability jobs; align with Caleb on platform conventions).
- Backfill window start/end controlled via config so we can do staged runs (e.g., 7d test, then 30d, then 90d).
- Query PromQL range queries in bounded chunks (e.g., 6h chunks) to avoid hammering Mimir and to respect query limits.
- Normalize labels to the public taxonomy:
  - For metrics missing component_id, use mapping rules from ENG-4830 (service->component) and tag the resulting rows with data_quality_flags=missing_component_label.
  - For known historical label changes (e.g., embeddings misclassification fixed in redwood/pr-19563), run a corrective recompute for affected time ranges.
- Idempotency:
  - Use MERGE semantics in BigQuery by (component_id, region, capability, time_bucket)
  - Keep a run_log table with job_run_id, window, success/failure, row counts, and a hash of the registry version used

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Backfill completes successfully for at least 30 days in staging, and 90 days in prod without exceeding Mimir query budget.
- [ ] For a known incident (e.g., 2025-11-07 US-East streaming degradation), we can query rollups and get:
      - peak error rate / disconnect rate
      - p95 latency delta vs baseline
      - duration above threshold
- [ ] ENG-4823 can consume these rollups without bespoke per-incident PromQL.
- [ ] Data quality flags appear for cases with missing labels, and the count of flagged rows is tracked in a dashboard.

## Monitoring
- Job success/failure + runtime metrics
- Mimir query volume + rate limiting errors
- Backfill coverage dashboard: % of components w/ rollups per day, broken down by region/capability

## Risks / open questions
- Cost: BigQuery storage and scan costs if we store 1m rollups for 90d for all components/regions. Mitigation: store 5m rollups for long window; keep 1m only for last 14d.
- Label completeness: older metrics may not have component_id tags. Mitigation: mapping fallback + clear data_quality_flags.
- Historical correctness: instrumentation bugs (e.g., embeddings vs chat classification) may require targeted recompute windows.

## Implementation tasks (proposed)
1) Finalize schema + dataset location (sync w/ Allison Grant + Aisha Bello)
2) Implement backfill runner w/ config-driven windows + chunking
3) Implement label normalization + mapping fallback integration (ENG-4830)
4) BigQuery write path w/ MERGE + run logs
5) Dashboards for coverage + data quality
6) Dry run in staging, then prod ramp (7d -> 30d -> 90d)

## Links
- Project brief: Status page transparency upgrades (Confluence)
- Component registry schema: observability-pack/pr-19371
- Component tags on gateway metrics: redwood/pr-19502
- Region-aware streaming metrics: redwood/pr-19518
- Incident timeline export hook: redwood/pr-19540
- Misclassified embeddings hotfix: redwood/pr-19563

- clustering: component_id, region, capability

If BigQuery is blocked for cost/latency, alternate is ClickHouse (but that complicates integrations). Assume BigQuery for v1.

## Backfill approach
- Implement as a scheduled batch job (Airflow/Prefect—use whatever we’re already running for observability jobs; align with Caleb on platform conventions).
- Backfill window start/end controlled via config so we can do staged runs (e.g., 7d test, then 30d, then 90d).
- Query PromQL range queries in bounded chunks (e.g., 6h chunks) to avoid hammering Mimir and to respect query limits.
- Normalize labels to the public taxonomy:
  - For metrics missing component_id, use mapping rules from ENG-4830 (service->component) and tag the resulting rows with data_quality_flags=missing_component_label.
  - For known historical label changes (e.g., embeddings misclassification fixed in redwood/pr-19563), run a corrective recompute for affected time ranges.
- Idempotency:
  - Use MERGE semantics in BigQuery by (component_id, region, capability, time_bucket)
  - Keep a run_log table with job_run_id, window, success/failure, row counts, and a hash of the registry version used

## Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Backfill completes successfully for at least 30 days in staging, and 90 days in prod without exceeding Mimir query budget.
- [ ] For a known incident (e.g., 2025-11-07 US-East streaming degradation), we can query rollups and get:
      - peak error rate / disconnect rate
      - p95 latency delta vs baseline
      - duration above threshold
- [ ] ENG-4823 can consume these rollups without bespoke per-incident PromQL.
- [ ] Data quality flags appear for cases with missing labels, and the count of flagged rows is tracked in a dashboard.

## Monitoring
- Job success/failure + runtime metrics
- Mimir query volume + rate limiting errors
- Backfill coverage dashboard: % of components w/ rollups per day, broken down by region/capability

## Risks / open questions
- Cost: BigQuery storage and scan costs if we store 1m rollups for 90d for all components/regions. Mitigation: store 5m rollups for long window; keep 1m only for last 14d.
- Label completeness: older metrics may not have component_id tags. Mitigation: mapping fallback + clear data_quality_flags.
- Historical correctness: instrumentation bugs (e.g., embeddings vs chat classification) may require targeted recompute windows.

## Implementation tasks (proposed)
1) Finalize schema + dataset location (sync w/ Allison Grant + Aisha Bello)
2) Implement backfill runner w/ config-driven windows + chunking
3) Implement label normalization + mapping fallback integration (ENG-4830)
4) BigQuery write path w/ MERGE + run logs
5) Dashboards for coverage + data quality
6) Dry run in staging, then prod ramp (7d -> 30d -> 90d)

## Links
- Project brief: Status page transparency upgrades (Confluence)
- Component registry schema: observability-pack/pr-19371
- Component tags on gateway metrics: redwood/pr-19502
- Region-aware streaming metrics: redwood/pr-19518
- Incident timeline export hook: redwood/pr-19540
- Misclassified embeddings hotfix: redwood/pr-19563

title: Lightweight approval workflow for customer-facing status updates during Sev0/Sev1 incidents
preview:
Goal
Implement a lightweight approval workflow for customer-facing Statuspage updates when incident severity is high (Sev0/Sev1). This is part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative and is intended to reduce "unreviewed" public messaging during fast-moving events while keeping time-to-first-update (TTFU) low.

Problem
We’ve repeatedly seen two failure modes during high-severity incidents:
1) Updates go out quickly but contain incorrect component/region scope, overly certain root-cause language, or customer-sensitive detail.
2) Teams delay posting because they’re unsure who should sign off, which increases tickets and erodes trust.

Non-goals
- Build a heavyweight change-approval system or require approvals for Sev2+.
- Require approvals for internal-only updates (e.g., #incidents threads).
- Replace Comms Lead ownership; the workflow should support Comms Lead, not create a new gate.

Scope
Add an optional approval step in incident-bot when publishing status updates under these conditions:
- Severity is Sev0 or Sev1 AND
- The update is being posted to the public status page (Statuspage API) AND
- The incident is in phases most likely to carry ambiguity/sensitivity (Investigating / Identified / Mitigating). (Resolved updates can be exempt; configurable.)

Approval policy (initial)
- Approver can be either: Incident Commander (IC) OR Support lead on duty (or named Support approver) depending on incident type.
- Default: require approval from IC for Sev0; require approval from either IC or Support lead for Sev1.
- Escape hatch: IC may override requirement with an explicit "bypass" action + reason (logged), only allowed for Sev0/Sev1 when TTFU SLO is at risk.

User experience (Slack)
- Comms Lead triggers "Publish status update" from incident-bot.
- If approval required, incident-bot posts an interactive message in #incidents (and DM to approver) with:
  - Draft text
  - Affected components/regions
  - Incident phase + severity
  - Buttons: Approve & Publish / Request Changes / Bypass (IC only)
  - Text input for reviewer notes and/or bypass reason
- After approve, incident-bot publishes via Statuspage client and logs:
  - approver identity
  - timestamp
  - draft hash (or message id)
  - what changed (phase/component list)

Implementation notes
- Keep approval step "thin": no new services. Implement inside incident-bot workflow + existing Statuspage API client.
- Store approval metadata in incident timeline events (same stream used for post-incident summary generation) so we can audit later.
- Make rules configurable (YAML / config) by severity + update phase. Default rules above.

Acceptance criteria
- Approval is enforced for Sev0/Sev1 public status updates based on configured rules.
- Approver set is clearly defined (IC and/or Support approver) and visible in the Slack prompt.
- Bypass option exists for IC with mandatory reason; bypass is logged.
- Publishing remains idempotent (do not double-post on retries) and status update attempts have an audit log entry.
- Workflow does not block posting for >5 minutes without an explicit notification; after N minutes, bot pings approver and IC.
- Works for both manual "publish" and template-generated drafts.

Rollout plan
- Phase 0 (internal): enable approval workflow only for Sev0 in staging, then production with a small set of approvers (SRE + Support leads) and measure TTFU.
- Phase 1: extend to Sev1 once we confirm we’re not increasing time-to-first-update materially.
- Provide short training + update runbook references.

Metrics to watch
- Median TTFU for Sev0/Sev1 (target unchanged; approvals should not materially increase).
- # of bypasses (should be low; investigate if high).
- Support ticket volume citing status page accuracy during Sev0/Sev1.

Links / references
- Policy: PM-612 status page post-incident summaries & publication policy
- Related: ENG-4822 incident-bot statuspage integration
- Related: ENG-4821 status page component granularity
- PR: incident-bot/pr-19433-support-approval-step-for-sev0-sev1
- PR: incident-bot/pr-19405-statuspage-api-client-with-retry-and-audit-log

title: Auto-generate publishable post-incident summaries from incident timeline + metrics (with review workflow)
preview:
Problem
Customers (and Support/CS) are asking for consistent, fast, and actionable post-incident summaries. Today, writing publishable summaries is manual and slow, often blocked on reconstructing timelines and pulling metrics. This results in (a) delays and (b) inconsistent content/tone, and (c) occasional over-disclosure risk.

Goal
Generate a draft, publishable post-incident summary automatically from:
1) the incident timeline (state changes, mitigations, comms events), and
2) a small set of component/region health metrics (errors/latency + duration),
then route it through a lightweight review workflow before it is posted publicly.

Non-goals
- Not generating a full internal postmortem (deep causal chain, contributing factors, action items). This is for customer-facing summaries only.
- Not auto-publishing without a human review step.
- Not making root cause claims beyond what the IC/Comms Lead marks as “confirmed.”

Users / stakeholders
- Primary: Comms Lead, Incident Commander (IC), SRE on-call
- Reviewers/approvers: Support lead (Sev0/Sev1), Legal (as needed), Security (as needed)
- Stakeholders: Dev Patel, Jasmine Liu, Rachel Kim, Michael Grant, Naomi Feldman

Inputs / data sources (proposed)
- Incident-bot incident record:
  - severity (sev0–sev3)
  - impacted components + regions + capabilities (from new taxonomy fields)
  - timeline events (created/identified/mitigating/monitoring/resolved + manual notes)
  - mitigation markers (e.g., failover, rollback, capacity shift, cache flush)
- Metrics (by component_id + region + capability):
  - error rate (%), request failure count
  - latency p95/p99 (where relevant)
  - computed impact window (start/end) with anti-flap/hysteresis-aware rules
  - optional: “fallback_active” and “degraded vs outage” classification where available

Output format
- Markdown (customer-facing), stored as a draft artifact and optionally attached/linked to the Statuspage incident.
- Must conform to internal template sections:
  1) Summary (1–3 sentences)
  2) Customer impact (what customers experienced; components/regions)
  3) Timeline (UTC, key milestones)
  4) Root cause (high-level classification; only if confirmed)
  5) Mitigation & recovery (what we did)
  6) Prevention / follow-ups (high-level, no internal-only details)
  7) Where to get updates (link to incident + support guidance)

Confidentiality constraints (hard requirements)
- Do not mention customer names, account IDs, or customer-specific capacity allocations.
- Do not include internal hostnames, shard IDs, or provider ticket numbers.
- Avoid definitive root cause statements unless IC marks “confirmed_root_cause=true.” Otherwise use “We believe” / “We are still investigating” wording.
- Dedicated/Private: default to a scoped statement (“Dedicated control plane”) unless explicitly approved to mention more.

Review + publish workflow (proposed)
- Draft is generated automatically when incident enters Resolved.
- Draft is posted to the incident Slack thread + linked in the incident tool.
- Required reviewers:
  - Sev0/Sev1: IC + Comms Lead + Support lead approval gate (ties into ENG-4860).
  - Sev2/Sev3: IC + Comms Lead (Support optional).
  - Legal/Security: optional gate triggered by flags (e.g., security-related label).
- After approval, Comms Lead can publish to Statuspage as the “Post-incident summary” (or as the final incident update body, depending on Statuspage API limitations).

Acceptance criteria
- When an incident is marked Resolved, a summary draft is generated within 10 minutes.
- Draft includes: impacted components/regions, impact duration, a short timeline, and mitigation notes (if present).
- Draft follows safe-language rules (no customer identifiers; no internal system identifiers) and passes automated lint checks.
- Approval workflow is enforced for Sev0/Sev1 before public posting.
- Supports “public” vs “internal-only” output modes (Private incidents should default to internal-only unless explicitly overridden).
- Backtesting: running the generator against at least 3 recent incidents produces drafts that Comms Leads rate as “usable with minor edits.”

Implementation plan
1) Data model + schema
- Define a minimal “summary input” schema:
  - incident_id, title, severity, start/end, detected_at
  - affected_components[] (component_id, display_name, region, capability)
  - timeline_events[] (timestamp, type, summary)
  - metrics_summary[] (component_id, region, error_peak, latency_peak, request_volume)
  - flags: confirmed_root_cause, security_related, customer_data_risk
- Add validation + defaults (e.g., if end missing, infer from resolved_at; if metrics missing, omit metrics paragraph).

2) Draft generator
- Implement a deterministic template engine (no LLM generation in v1) to reduce disclosure risk and ensure consistent tone.
- Provide controlled phrasing for unknowns (“Investigating”), confirmed fixes, and monitoring.
- Include a small “tone guide” (short sentences, no blame, no vendor callouts).

3) Metrics fetcher
- Query component health panels (or a metrics API wrapper) for the impacted component_id/region in the computed impact window.
- If metrics are unavailable, generate summary without metrics and include “We observed elevated error rates” only if an alert/timeline event supports it.

4) Linting + policy checks
- Blocklist patterns (customer identifiers, internal domains, cluster names).
- Length constraints (e.g., Summary <= 400 chars).
- Root cause gating (only render the root cause section if confirmed).

5) Review workflow integration
- Wire into incident-bot workflow:
  - generate draft on Resolved
  - post to Slack thread
  - require approvals per severity (align with ENG-4860)
  - log approver + publish timestamp in audit log

6) Rollout
- Feature flag: post_incident_summary_autodraft
- Dogfood: SRE + Infra on-call for 2 weeks
- Limited public rollout: Hosted API incidents only
- Full rollout: include Console + Dedicated control-plane components

Testing
- Unit tests: template rendering (golden snapshots), lint checks, schema validation.
- Integration tests: replay a synthetic incident with mocked timeline + metrics; ensure draft is produced and approvals enforced.
- Manual QA: run generator on recent incidents (including a partial region streaming degradation) and collect edits required.

Links / references
- Project: Status page transparency upgrades
- Policy alignment: PM-612 (post-incident summaries policy)
- Related work:
  - ENG-4822 incident-bot statuspage integration (draft/push updates)
  - ENG-4830 internal service → status component mapping
  - ENG-4860 approval workflow for customer-facing updates
  - ENG-4855 metrics backfill job (improves timelines/graphs)
- Implementing PRs to align with:
  - incident timeline export hook (redwood PR-19540)
  - status summary metadata fields in incident events (redwood PR-19601)

Open questions
- Where should the summary draft be stored as the source-of-truth (incident-bot DB vs. GitHub artifact vs. Statuspage-only)?
- Do we want a single summary per incident or allow multiple revisions (with a visible “Last updated”)?
- Dedicated/Private policy: do we always publish a summary if customers were impacted but the public status page is not scoped to them?

Risks
- Metrics mismatch could create credibility issues; mitigate via conservative phrasing and requiring at least one corroborating signal (alert/timeline entry).
- Over-disclosure: mitigate via deterministic templates + linting + approval gates.
- On-call burden: mitigate by making drafts “good enough” and minimizing required manual fields.

title: Implement status page component granularity (new taxonomy + shared registry)
preview:
Goal
Implement the new public status page component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability where applicable) as a shared, versioned registry/config and ensure consistent usage across:
- incident intake + comms (incident-bot)
- observability labels/dashboards + SLO tooling
- status page publishing (Statuspage component IDs and grouping)

Why
Recent incidents (e.g., US-East streaming degradation) exposed that our current status components are too coarse ("API") which led to inaccurate/misleading public updates and increased Support load. Customers have explicitly asked for per-surface components (Streaming vs Chat vs Embeddings) and per-region visibility.

Scope
1) Component registry / source-of-truth
- Create a single registry file that defines:
  - stable component_id (machine ID)
  - display_name (public label)
  - product_area (hosted-api | console | dedicated | private)
  - capability (chat | streaming | embeddings | reranking | control-plane) where relevant
  - region (us-east | us-west | eu-west | ap-southeast) where relevant
  - ownership (team/oncall rotation for internal routing only; not published)
  - rollout flags (e.g., enabled_public=true/false) for staged rollout
- Registry must be validated in CI via schema to prevent drift (naming conventions, required fields, uniqueness).

2) Statuspage component mapping
- Map registry entries to Statuspage component IDs (the provider object ID) and groupings.
- Provide a deterministic mapping layer so incident-bot and dashboards refer to component_id and never hardcode provider IDs.

3) Tooling consumption
- Update incident-bot intake fields to use component_id + region + capability from the registry.
- Ensure observability/SLO labels align with component_id/region/capability (for burn alerts and dashboards).
- Add lightweight docs for engineers/on-call: how to pick the right component(s), edge cases (partial region impact, streaming-only), and what NOT to do (don’t mark whole Hosted API if only embeddings are impacted).

Non-goals
- Defining the content/tone of incident updates (tracked separately in comms template work).
- Implementing the internal service -> component inference logic (tracked in ENG-4830).
- Console UI changes for surfacing incidents/summaries (tracked under Console/Design tickets).

Implementation notes / decisions
- Source-of-truth lives in repo config (not Confluence) per ADR-014, with Confluence serving as the human-readable reference.
- component_id format: <surface>.<capability>[.<region>] where region is included only when the public page is region-split for that surface.
  Examples:
  - hosted.chat.us-east
  - hosted.streaming.eu-west
  - hosted.embeddings.us-west
  - console (global)
  - dedicated.control-plane (global)
- Staged rollout: land registry + validation first; then enable public components behind an allowlist/flag; then flip default.

Acceptance criteria
- A registry/config exists with schema validation and is used as the canonical list of public components.
- incident-bot can only select valid component_ids; updates reference the correct public components.
- Observability dashboards/alerts use consistent labels (component_id, region, capability) and link to runbooks.
- No hardcoded component strings in incident-bot/alerting configs (other than controlled display names).
- Dry-run: simulate an incident with streaming-only impact in us-east and confirm:
  - status page marks only the streaming component for us-east
  - dashboards show matching impact
  - audit job does not flag mismatch

Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (internal): registry landed + CI validation; incident-bot intake updated; on-call training.
- Phase 2 (limited public): enable new components for Hosted surfaces in us-east only; monitor for mapping bugs.
- Phase 3 (full): enable all target regions/surfaces; announce externally.

References
- Confluence taxonomy: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comms/status-page-component-taxonomy.json
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-014-status-page-component-mapping-source-of-truth.json
- Signal standards: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/customer-facing-status-signals-standard.json

Related PRs (expected / landed)
- observability-pack PR-19371 (schema + registry validation)
- incident-bot PR-19321 (incident intake fields: component/region/capability)
- slo-toolkit PR-19211 (standardize SLO labels)

Open questions
- Do we split Hosted Chat vs Hosted API Gateway as separate public components? (Current proposal: keep public at capability level; gateway stays internal.)
- Dedicated/Private incidents: confirm which components are visible publicly vs only in Console/contractual reporting (align with PM-612).
text:
Goal
Implement the new public status page component taxonomy (product surface x region x capability where applicable) as a shared, versioned registry/config and ensure consistent usage across:
- incident intake + comms (incident-bot)
- observability labels/dashboards + SLO tooling
- status page publishing (Statuspage component IDs and grouping)

Why
Recent incidents (e.g., US-East streaming degradation) exposed that our current status components are too coarse ("API") which led to inaccurate/misleading public updates and increased Support load. Customers have explicitly asked for per-surface components (Streaming vs Chat vs Embeddings) and per-region visibility.

Scope
1) Component registry / source-of-truth
- Create a single registry file that defines:
  - stable component_id (machine ID)
  - display_name (public label)
  - product_area (hosted-api | console | dedicated | private)
  - capability (chat | streaming | embeddings | reranking | control-plane) where relevant
  - region (us-east | us-west | eu-west | ap-southeast) where relevant
  - ownership (team/oncall rotation for internal routing only; not published)
  - rollout flags (e.g., enabled_public=true/false) for staged rollout
- Registry must be validated in CI via schema to prevent drift (naming conventions, required fields, uniqueness).

2) Statuspage component mapping
- Map registry entries to Statuspage component IDs (the provider object ID) and groupings.
- Provide a deterministic mapping layer so incident-bot and dashboards refer to component_id and never hardcode provider IDs.

3) Tooling consumption
- Update incident-bot intake fields to use component_id + region + capability from the registry.
- Ensure observability/SLO labels align with component_id/region/capability (for burn alerts and dashboards).
- Add lightweight docs for engineers/on-call: how to pick the right component(s), edge cases (partial region impact, streaming-only), and what NOT to do (don’t mark whole Hosted API if only embeddings are impacted).

Non-goals
- Defining the content/tone of incident updates (tracked separately in comms template work).
- Implementing the internal service -> component inference logic (tracked in ENG-4830).
- Console UI changes for surfacing incidents/summaries (tracked under Console/Design tickets).

Implementation notes / decisions
- Source-of-truth lives in repo config (not Confluence) per ADR-014, with Confluence serving as the human-readable reference.
- component_id format: <surface>.<capability>[.<region>] where region is included only when the public page is region-split for that surface.
  Examples:
  - hosted.chat.us-east
  - hosted.streaming.eu-west
  - hosted.embeddings.us-west
  - console (global)
  - dedicated.control-plane (global)
- Staged rollout: land registry + validation first; then enable public components behind an allowlist/flag; then flip default.

Acceptance criteria
- A registry/config exists with schema validation and is used as the canonical list of public components.
- incident-bot can only select valid component_ids; updates reference the correct public components.
- Observability dashboards/alerts use consistent labels (component_id, region, capability) and link to runbooks.
- No hardcoded component strings in incident-bot/alerting configs (other than controlled display names).
- Dry-run: simulate an incident with streaming-only impact in us-east and confirm:
  - status page marks only the streaming component for us-east
  - dashboards show matching impact
  - audit job does not flag mismatch

Rollout plan
- Phase 1 (internal): registry landed + CI validation; incident-bot intake updated; on-call training.
- Phase 2 (limited public): enable new components for Hosted surfaces in us-east only; monitor for mapping bugs.
- Phase 3 (full): enable all target regions/surfaces; announce externally.

References
- Confluence taxonomy: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/status-page-and-comms/status-page-component-taxonomy.json
- ADR: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-014-status-page-component-mapping-source-of-truth.json
- Signal standards: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/observability-standards/customer-facing-status-signals-standard.json

Related PRs (expected / landed)
- observability-pack PR-19371 (schema + registry validation)
- incident-bot PR-19321 (incident intake fields: component/region/capability)
- slo-toolkit PR-19211 (standardize SLO labels)

Open questions
- Do we split Hosted Chat vs Hosted API Gateway as separate public components? (Current proposal: keep public at capability level; gateway stays internal.)
- Dedicated/Private incidents: confirm which components are visible publicly vs only in Console/contractual reporting (align with PM-612).

title: Map internal services/metrics to public status page components (registry + routing rules + tests)
preview:
We need a deterministic mapping layer from internal service identifiers + metric tags to the canonical public status components (product surface x region x capability), so incident-bot + dashboards + audit tooling can consistently mark the correct components during incidents.

This ticket implements:
- A component registry lookup (component_id, display name, ownership) used as the output contract
- A mapping function from internal signals (gateway/service names, request routes, model/task type, region) -> component_id(s)
- Tests + fixtures to prevent regressions and catch "embeddings misclassified as chat" style bugs before they hit the status page

Background:
Recent incidents showed we can’t rely on manual component selection when services are shared and failure modes are capability-specific (e.g., streaming disconnects only). We also need consistent behavior across:
- gateway metrics tags
- SLO burn alerts keyed by component
- incident-bot intake fields and draft generation

Non-goals:
- Choosing thresholds/anti-flap behavior (handled in observability-pack / signals standard)
- Statuspage API client implementation (incident-bot already doing this)

Owners / reviewers:
- DRI: Ravi Menon (service-to-component mapping)
- Reviewers: Mina Haddad (routing/region taxonomy), Logan Wright (telemetry mapping), Aisha Bello (SLO dashboards), Christine Wong (status transparency implementation)

---
Plan
1) Define mapping inputs + precedence
- Primary inputs (in order of preference):
  a) Explicit component tags emitted by the API gateway / runtime metrics (component_id)
  b) Derived mapping from route/capability tags + region (capability: chat|streaming|embeddings|reranking; region: us-east|us-west|eu-west)
  c) Fallback mapping from internal service name (gateway, scheduler, tokenization, vector-store) to a best-effort component group

- Precedence rule: explicit component_id tag wins; if missing, derive; if ambiguous, return "unknown" and require manual selection.

2) Implement mapping module + registry contract
- New module: status_components/mapping (language: same as existing incident tooling config; keep pure + side-effect free)
- Registry is source-of-truth and validated by schema (see registry schema work in observability-pack)
- Output: list of component_id(s) to allow multi-component incidents (e.g., Hosted API + Console) but avoid fan-out when evidence is weak

3) Add test suite and fixtures
- Table-driven tests covering:
  - Hosted API chat vs embeddings vs reranking classification
  - Streaming-specific classification using streaming error/disconnect tags
  - Region-only impact: component_id should include region scoping when tags provide it
  - Shared infra signals (scheduler): should not automatically mark all components unless there’s correlated gateway evidence
  - Regression tests for known prior bug: embeddings errors incorrectly mapped to chat

4) Wire into incident-bot + audits
- incident-bot intake fields already capture component/region/capability; mapping should power suggestions + validation
- weekly status accuracy audit job should use the same mapping library (or same config) to compare incident tags vs public updates

---
Mapping rules (initial)
Public component grouping (example IDs; must match registry):
- hosted-api.us-east.chat
- hosted-api.us-east.streaming
- hosted-api.us-east.embeddings
- hosted-api.us-east.reranking
- hosted-api.us-west.*
- hosted-api.eu-west.*
- console (global)
- dedicated.control-plane (global)

Rules:
- If metric/event includes component_id tag that exists in registry => use it.
- Else, derive from:
  - region tag => us-east/us-west/eu-west (required for Hosted API components)
  - capability tag:
    - streaming: request.mode=stream or endpoint=/v1/chat/completions with stream=true
    - embeddings: endpoint=/v1/embeddings or task=embeddings
    - reranking: endpoint=/v1/rerank or task=rerank
    - chat/text generation: default for /v1/chat/completions or /v1/completions
- If only service_name is present:
  - gateway => do not infer capability; return unknown unless route/capability also present
  - scheduler/runtime => never infer public component without correlated gateway evidence; return unknown

Anti-footgun:
- Never map a single internal service to multiple public components unless the event includes explicit route/capability evidence. Avoid the "mark everything degraded" failure mode.

---
Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Mapping module returns stable component_ids for all core Hosted API capabilities across 3 regions (us-east/us-west/eu-west)
- [ ] Unit tests cover at least 20 representative cases including the known embeddings/chat regression
- [ ] CI fails on unknown component_id references (registry mismatch) and on mapping ambiguity without explicit override
- [ ] Incident-bot can show mapped component suggestions in intake and uses them for draft updates when confidence is high

---
Implementation notes / links
Related work:
- ENG-4821 (taxonomy + registry rollout)
- ENG-4841 (component-level SLO burn alerts)

Related PRs (context / dependencies):
- gateway emits component tags: sources/github/redwood/pr-19502-emit-component-tags-on-api-gateway-metrics.json
- streaming region-aware error metrics: sources/github/redwood/pr-19518-add-region-aware-streaming-error-metrics.json
- embeddings misclassification bug fix (use as regression test seed): sources/github/redwood/pr-19563-fix-misclassified-embeddings-errors-in-component-map.json
- registry schema + validation: sources/github/observability-pack/pr-19371-add-status-component-registry-json-schema.json

Open questions
- Dedicated + Private: for now we only expose Dedicated control-plane as a single public component. Do not attempt to map per-customer Dedicated incidents via this layer.
- Console vs API: Console incidents should be mapped via console service tags (global) and never inferred from gateway metrics.

Rollout
- Stage 1: ship mapping + tests behind "suggestions only" in incident-bot (no auto-publish)
- Stage 2: enable auto-fill for Sev1+ with Comms Lead confirmation
- Stage 3: feed weekly accuracy audit using the mapping output

Risks
- Missing tags in metrics => mapping returns unknown; mitigation is instrumentation (gateway tags) and clear on-call guidance.
- Over-broad mapping could cause unnecessary customer alarm; mitigate by requiring capability evidence.

---
Progress updates
2025-11-18 (Ravi): Drafted rule precedence + test matrix. Need Mina to confirm region taxonomy keys and fallbacks.
2025-11-22 (Logan): Confirmed gateway will emit component_id in success + error counters; still missing for some 4xx paths.
2025-11-26 (Mina): Region keys should be canonical: us-east, us-west, eu-west. No "global" for Hosted API.
2025-12-03 (Ravi): Implemented initial mapping function + 14 tests. Added regression test for embeddings misclassification. Remaining: wire into incident-bot suggestions + add more streaming edge cases (disconnect spikes without elevated 5xx).

title: Add component-level SLO burn-rate alerts aligned to public status page components
preview:
## Context
As part of the Status page transparency upgrades initiative, we’re tightening the loop between internal health signals and customer-facing status updates.

Today, burn alerts are generally service-centric (gateway, scheduler, etc.), which slows down:
- time-to-detection (we often detect “gateway SLO” but need manual translation to “Streaming (US-East) is degraded”)
- time-to-first-update (TTFU) because Comms Lead/IC need extra verification and component selection

This ticket adds component-level SLO burn-rate alerts keyed to the *public status page component taxonomy* (component_id + region + capability where applicable) so on-call can quickly understand customer impact and choose the right status components.

## Goal
Create and route burn-rate alerts that match public status components (Hosted API, Streaming, Embeddings, Reranking, Console where applicable), split by region for key regions, and annotate alerts with links to the status page update runbook + component dashboards.

These alerts are not meant to auto-update the status page directly (that’s handled by incident-bot integration + comms workflow), but they should be strong “this is customer-impacting” signals.

## Non-goals
- Defining the full public component taxonomy (tracked in ENG-4821 / Confluence taxonomy doc)
- Implementing statuspage publishing automation (ENG-4822)
- Building new SLOs from scratch for every internal service; we will key alerts off existing SLO signals where possible and add missing labels/instrumentation separately

## Alerting model
We will implement standard multi-window, multi-burn alerts (SRE playbook style) per component:
- Fast burn: 5m/1h windows (urgent, page)
- Slow burn: 30m/6h windows (ticket/page depending on component)

Signals:
- error-rate SLOs for request success (primary)
- latency SLOs only where we have stable percentiles and clear impact thresholds, and only if we can avoid flapping (see Observability standard)

Key labels (must match public taxonomy / mapping rules):
- component_id (e.g., hosted-api, streaming, embeddings, reranking)
- region (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-west)
- capability (optional; e.g., streaming vs non-streaming for Hosted API paths where needed)

## Scope (v1)
Component-level burn alerts for:
1) Hosted API
   - us-east, us-west, eu-west
   - success-rate burn (gateway + downstream combined where we already compute request success)
2) Streaming
   - us-east, us-west, eu-west
   - success-rate burn using streaming disconnect/error counters where available
3) Embeddings
   - us-east, eu-west
4) Reranking
   - us-east, eu-west

Notes:
- Dedicated / Private: we will not expose customer-specific capacity issues via public status, but we still want internal component-aligned alerting. v1 will keep routing internal-only and avoid implying a public component state for Dedicated/Private. (Dedicated control-plane signals tracked separately by Ethan.)

## Implementation plan
1) Standardize SLO label set
   - Ensure SLO definitions in slo-toolkit carry component_id/region/capability labels consistent with the public component registry.
   - Follow naming conventions agreed with Observability (Allison) to keep dashboards/alerts aligned.

2) Add burn-rate alerts keyed by component
   - Add alert rules in slo-toolkit for each (component_id, region[, capability]) pair.
   - Define consistent alert names, e.g.:
     - "SLO Burn: streaming us-east (fast)"
     - "SLO Burn: embeddings eu-west (slow)"

3) Annotations + runbook links
   Each alert must include:
   - Link to component health dashboard (per-region view)
   - Link to status-page update runbook
   - Short “what to do next” snippet (verify impact, pick status components, start incident if needed)

4) Routing + noise controls
   - Fast burn pages SRE on-call (PagerDuty) for Hosted API + Streaming.
   - Slow burn creates ticket / non-page notification for lower-sev components (Embeddings/Reranking) unless sustained.
   - Apply anti-flap/hysteresis guidance from the Customer-facing status signals standard.

5) Rollout
   - Week 1: shadow mode (alerts to #eng-sre-alerts only) to evaluate noise + label correctness.
   - Week 2: enable paging for Streaming + Hosted API fast burn only.
   - Week 3: expand paging/notification coverage if stable.

## Acceptance criteria
- [x] For each in-scope component/region pair, an SLO burn alert exists (fast + slow windows where appropriate)
- [x] Alerts include component_id/region labels and match the status component taxonomy naming
- [x] Alert annotations include links to (a) component dashboard, (b) status-page update runbook
- [x] Paging routes correctly to SRE on-call for Hosted API + Streaming fast burn
- [x] Shadow/noise review completed with at least 1 week of data; noisy alerts tuned or disabled
- [x] Documented in the on-call channel (what changed, how to interpret)

## Risks / open questions
- Label correctness depends on upstream telemetry tagging. If we see mismatches, we need follow-up instrumentation work (e.g., gateway metrics missing region tags).
- Latency-based degradations: product wants to reflect “degraded performance” cases, but latency signals tend to flap. v1 will focus on error-rate burns; consider adding latency-only burns after anti-flap logic is proven.

## Links
- Observability standard: customer-facing status signals + thresholds
- Status-page update runbook (for annotations)
- Component dashboards spec

## Deliverables
- PR(s) in slo-toolkit adding component burn alerts
- Validation notes from shadow rollout (noise, false positives, missing labels)
- On-call announcement blurb

title: SRE dashboards + alerting for overload protection (load shedding + breakers)
preview:
Goal
Ship first-class SRE dashboards + alert rules for the Hosted overload protection system (circuit breakers + load shedding), and wire them into oncall paging with a structured false-positive review loop.

Context
As we roll out admission control (gateway) + routing fallback + runtime backpressure, we need consistent visibility and actionable paging to prevent “silent overload” (tail latency + timeouts) and to avoid alert noise. Dashboards should support both: (1) real-time incident triage and (2) post-incident/threshold tuning.

Scope (this ticket)
1) Dashboards (Grafana) in observability-pack
- Overload overview dashboard (by region):
  - Shed rate (% and RPS) broken down by reason bucket (gateway admission vs runtime saturation vs dependency health) with low-cardinality labels.
  - Breaker state transitions (closed/open/half-open) rate and current open count.
  - Correlated request health: p95/p99 latency, 5xx, timeouts, and 429/503 overload error rates.
  - Capacity/saturation signals: GPU scheduler backlog, prefill/decode queue depth, token/sec saturation (aggregated), and KV/memory pressure proxy.
- Tenant/tier drilldown dashboard (guarded):
  - Top affected tenants by shed rate (capped list; avoid high-cardinality tenant_id in alerting).
  - Tier rollup (self-serve vs enterprise Hosted vs Dedicated) to confirm tier-aware policy behavior.
- Runbook + annotation integration:
  - Dashboard links to oncall runbook: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/hosted-overload-circuit-breaker-runbook.json
  - Annotations for rollouts/toggles and breaker canary enablement.

2) Alerts (Prometheus rules) + paging
- Alerts must be SLO-informed and “actionable” (each alert has a linked runbook and clear next steps).
- Initial alert set (proposed):
  - P0: Regional overload shedding sustained high
    - Trigger: shed_rate > 2% for 10m AND request_rate > baseline_min
    - Page: #eng-oncall (PagerDuty service: Hosted API - Core)
  - P0: Breaker open storms
    - Trigger: breaker_open_transitions_rate above threshold for 5m OR breaker_open_count > N for 10m
    - Page: #eng-oncall
  - P1: Missing Retry-After regression
    - Trigger: overload_responses_without_retry_after > 0.5% for 15m (route-grouped)
    - Page during canary only; otherwise ticket + Slack notification
  - P1: Saturation signals exceed safe band without shedding (risk of tail latency blowup)
    - Trigger: runtime_queue_depth high + shed_rate ~0 + p99 latency rising
    - Page: #eng-oncall (indicates breaker thresholds may be too lax or signals failing)
  - P2: Metrics pipeline gaps
    - Trigger: no samples for critical overload metrics in region for 10m
    - Notify: #observability-infra (no page unless during canary)

3) False-positive review loop (process + plumbing)
- Add a lightweight review checklist for any page caused by these alerts:
  - Was this true overload? (correlate with p99, queue depth, error taxonomy rates)
  - Did policy behave as expected (tier-aware, tenant isolation)?
  - Was the alert threshold too aggressive or missing a “traffic present” guard?
  - Action taken + outcome
- Implementation:
  - Create a shared “Overload Alert Review” note template in the runbook (link in alert annotations).
  - For first 2 weeks of canary: daily 15-min review (Aisha + Christine Wong + oncall) and adjust thresholds.
  - After GA: weekly review of alert firing frequency and top noisy rules.

Non-goals
- Defining new saturation metrics (handled in ENG-4825) beyond what’s needed to build meaningful dashboards.
- Building customer-facing dashboards (Support/CS enablement tracked elsewhere).

Deliverables
- observability-pack PR with dashboards + alerts: sources/github/observability-pack/pr-913-overload-shedding-dashboards-and-alerts.json
- Dashboard spec alignment: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/overload-protection-dashboards.json
- Runbook links included in every alert (annotation / labels)
- Paging routing validated with a controlled test (synthetic / staging / canary)

Acceptance criteria
- Dashboards exist in prod and are discoverable under “Hosted API → Overload Protection”.
- Alerts fire in staging/canary under induced overload and page the correct oncall rotation.
- Alerts do not page on idle/low-traffic regions (guardrails included).
- For canary region, oncall can answer within 5 minutes:
  - Are we shedding? Why? Which layer (gateway/routing/runtime)?
  - Is this localized (region) or systemic?
  - What are the safe mitigations (toggle/cap/rollback) and where are they documented?
- False-positive review process documented and first week of reviews completed with threshold adjustments logged.

Notes / implementation constraints
- Keep label cardinality bounded in alerting metrics. Tenant_id only allowed in dashboards with top-K queries; avoid tenant_id in alert labels.
- Prefer ratio-based alerts with “traffic present” gating to prevent noise.
- Coordinate with Telemetry Platform (Logan Wright) for any missing metrics, and with Runtime team for signal semantics.

Primary collaborators
- Aisha Bello (owner)
- Christine Wong (overload protection engineer)
- Logan Wright (telemetry)
- Allison Grant (alerting standards)
- Rafael Mendes (SRE lead)

Links
- Confluence dashboard spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/overload-protection-dashboards.json
- Oncall runbook: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/runbooks/hosted-overload-circuit-breaker-runbook.json
- Runtime signal notes: sources/confluence/eng-serving-runtime/runtime-architecture/runtime-backpressure-and-saturation-signals.json
- Related PRs: sources/github/observability-pack/pr-913-overload-shedding-dashboards-and-alerts.json, sources/github/incident-bot/pr-544-incident-bot-announces-breaker-state-changes.json

title: Run KMS outage game day (timeout/throttling/AccessDenied) + capture follow-ups
preview:
## Goal
Validate end-to-end behavior and operator readiness when KMS is degraded or misconfigured across Hosted/Dedicated/Private deployment modes.

We want to confirm:
- KMS wrapper timeouts/retry budgets/circuit breaker prevent retry storms + cascading failures
- Error taxonomy is consistent (logs/metrics), and on-call can quickly triage
- Dashboards/alerts are actionable (not noisy) and point to correct remediation
- Runbooks cover the most common failure modes with concrete steps
- Private installer preflight checks catch the “obvious” misconfigs before customers hit runtime failures

## Scope / systems under test
- Control plane: key version config/dual-read cutover flags
- Data plane: envelope decrypt path (runtime)
- Observability: KMS dependency health dashboard + alerts
- Private installer: KMS preflight validation (where available)

References:
- Confluence game day doc: sources/confluence/oncall-and-incident-response/game-days/kms-outage-game-day-plan-and-results-2025q4.json
- Near-miss learnings (retry storm): sources/slack/incidents/1747779033-near-miss-kms-retry-storm.json
- Prior incident (timeout spike us-east): sources/slack/incidents/1746508855-kms-timeout-spike-us-east.json
- Epic: sources/linear/engineering/ENG-4821-kms-integration-hardening-rotation-docs-epic.json

## Scenarios (run in order)
### Scenario A — Regional KMS latency + intermittent timeouts (most realistic)
**Injection:** Add 2–4s latency + 10–20% timeout rate for KMS calls in staging (us-east). Duration 20 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Wrapper enforces per-call timeout and bounded retries with jitter
- Circuit breaker opens after threshold; service returns controlled errors (no unbounded queue growth)
- No retry amplification; request rate to KMS drops once breaker opens
- Alerts trigger once and point to the correct dashboard panels

### Scenario B — KMS throttling (rate limiting)
**Injection:** Simulate throttling responses and/or reduce KMS quota to force 429/Throttling. Duration 15 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Backoff/jitter observed; no synchronized retry bursts
- Backpressure/throttle budget protects KMS and preserves overall system health
- Error taxonomy shows Throttling, not Timeout, and remediation guidance is consistent

### Scenario C — Permission regression / AccessDenied
**Injection:** Remove a required decrypt permission from the staging role (or swap to a role missing kms:Decrypt). Duration 10 minutes.
**Expected behavior:**
- Fail fast with AccessDenied classification (no retries)
- Operator-facing errors include missing action/principal hints
- Runbook leads on-call to confirm principal/ARN mismatch quickly

### Scenario D (optional if time) — Key disabled / wrong key version during cutover
**Injection:** Disable the “new” key version while dual-read is enabled; then attempt cutover.
**Expected behavior:**
- Dual-read continues to succeed via old key; cutover is blocked / rollback guidance is clear
- Audit log events emitted for toggles and failed cutover attempts

## Participants / roles
- Facilitator: Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Incident Commander (IC): Tomáš Novák (On-call readiness)
- Comms lead: Fiona McCarthy (Security Ops)
- Observers / scribes: Allison Grant (observability standards), Kevin Osei (security telemetry)
- Control plane support: Ethan Park / Benji Okafor
- Private deployments support: Vivek Kulkarni / Bruno Silva
- Security escalation: Marcus Lin / Hanae Suzuki

## Schedule
- Prep review (30 min): 2025-11-18 10:00–10:30 PT
- Game day execution (90 min): 2025-11-19 13:00–14:30 PT
- Hotwash (30 min): 2025-11-19 14:30–15:00 PT
- Follow-up triage + ticketing (30 min): 2025-11-20 10:00–10:30 PT

Zoom bridge: “SRE War Room” (calendar invite sent by Rafael)
Primary Slack channel: #eng-sre (threaded)
Secondary/executive updates (if needed): #incidents (no paging; informational only)

## Pre-req checklist (must be true before execution)
- [x] Staging environment selected + traffic generator ready
- [x] Fault injection mechanism confirmed (timeouts/latency/throttling/AccessDenied)
- [x] Dashboards deployed and readable for participants (ENG-4828)
- [x] Feature flags documented (dual-read/cutover toggles)
- [x] On-call runbook draft available (kms decrypt failures)

## Comms plan
- T-15 min: announce start in #eng-sre with link to dashboard + runbook
- During event: IC posts status updates every 10 minutes in the same thread (what’s injected, what we’re seeing, next step)
- If staging-only impact escalates unexpectedly (e.g., cross-environment spill): IC escalates to #incidents and pauses injections
- End of event: facilitator posts summary + initial action list and assigns owners

Template for updates (copy/paste):
- Time:
- Scenario:
- Injection details:
- Observed behavior (latency, error rate, breaker state):
- Alerts fired (yes/no, noisy/actionable):
- Operator experience (runbook usefulness):
- Decision (continue/pause/rollback):

## Success criteria
- No cascading failures (no sustained queue growth, no runaway retries)
- Circuit breaker behavior matches ADR-007 expectations (opens/closes predictably)
- Alerts are actionable (<= 1 page for each scenario; no alert storms)
- On-call can diagnose root cause within 10 minutes using dashboards/logs/runbook
- Clear remediation steps exist for AccessDenied + throttling

## Results (executed 2025-11-19)
### What worked
- Timeout/latency scenario triggered the expected KMS latency panels; traces clearly showed KMS spans as top contributor.
- AccessDenied was correctly classified (no retries), and the error message included missing permission hints.
- Circuit breaker prevented retry amplification; KMS request volume dropped after breaker opened.

### Gaps found
1) Alert noise: KMS latency alert fired repeatedly across regions because panels were not properly scoped by env/region.
2) Runbook gap: runbook lacked a “first 5 minutes” checklist and a direct log query for mapping error code -> taxonomy category.
3) Throttling remediation: unclear guidance on quota vs client-side backoff; needed a link to AWS quota request guidance for customer-managed KMS.
4) Private operator experience: preflight report format was helpful, but the error text for wrong region/key ARN could be more explicit.
5) Cutover safety: dual-read status wasn’t visible in the standard dashboard; required manual checking of config.

## Follow-up actions (created / linked)
- [x] Reduce retry amplification on timeouts (hotfix): sources/github/redwood/pr-18563-hotfix-reduce-kms-retry-storm-on-timeout.json
- [x] Add throttle budget/backpressure integration: sources/github/redwood/pr-18570-add-kms-throttle-budget-and-backpressure.json
- [ ] Tune alert thresholds + scope by env/region (owner: Allison Grant) — follow-up in ENG-4828 comments
- [ ] Update runbook with “first 5 minutes” triage checklist + log queries (owner: Tomáš Novák) — follow-up in sources/confluence/eng-sre/runbooks/kms-decrypt-failures-runbook.json
- [ ] Add dashboard panel for dual-read/cutover flag state (owner: Nadia Rahman) — to be filed as new ENG ticket
- [ ] Improve Private installer messaging for wrong region/key ARN (owner: Bruno Silva) — follow-up on sources/github/redwood-private-installer/pr-392-add-kms-preflight-validation-and-permission-checks.json

## Notes
- This game day is intended to be repeatable quarterly, especially before enabling rotation/cutover for new Private customers.
- Evidence artifacts captured: screenshots of dashboards during each scenario, Slack thread transcript, and a short hotwash summary added to the Confluence game day page.
- Related discussions:
  - Alert noise thread: sources/slack/eng-sre/1746280021-kms-latency-alerts-too-noisy.json
  - Scenario brainstorming: sources/slack/eng-sre/1747024911-game-day-scenarios-for-kms-outage.json

title: Incident-bot: Statuspage integration (draft + publish updates, capture impacted components)
preview:
Background
We’re upgrading public status page transparency (component granularity + consistent comms + publishable post-incident summaries). Today incident-bot helps with incident intake and internal coordination, but status updates are still largely manual and inconsistent (TTFU misses, incorrect component selection, copy drift).

This ticket adds a Statuspage integration into incident-bot so we can:
1) capture component/region/capability impact fields as part of incident intake, and keep them current
2) generate status update drafts (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved) from incident state + known facts
3) publish updates to Statuspage via API with audit logging + safe retry semantics
4) support an approval step for Sev0/Sev1 where required (Support lead or IC), without adding significant on-call burden

Goals
- Reduce time-to-first-update (TTFU) by making it easy to draft + publish a compliant first status update within target window.
- Improve correctness: ensure updates reference the right public components (product surface x region x capability) per the component registry.
- Standardize language: follow the incident communication templates and tone guidance.
- Create an internal audit log of what was published, by whom, and when (for later accuracy audits and post-incident summary generation).

Non-goals
- Auto-detect incidents or auto-change public component state directly from metrics (handled by observability signals/anti-flap work).
- Building a full Statuspage UI inside Slack.
- Publishing any customer-identifying details or capacity/internal architecture specifics.

Functional requirements
A) Incident intake fields (required)
- Add/require structured fields:
  - impacted_components: list of status component IDs (from registry)
  - impacted_regions: list (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-west)
  - capabilities: chat | streaming | embeddings | reranking | console | dedicated-control-plane (as applicable)
  - impact_type: degraded_performance | partial_outage | major_outage
  - customer_impact_summary: short, safe statement (no customer names)
- Validation:
  - component IDs must exist in registry
  - region/capability must be compatible with selected components (best-effort validation; warn if mismatch)

B) Draft generation
- Provide a /incident status draft command (or incident-bot button) that generates phase-specific text using templates:
  - Investigating
  - Identified
  - Mitigating
  - Monitoring
  - Resolved (includes pointer to post-incident summary follow-up, without committing to a specific timeframe)
- Draft should pull in:
  - incident start time (approx) if available
  - impact_type and affected components/regions
  - any selected mitigation actions from the incident timeline (if recorded)
  - safe metrics snippets if available (e.g., “elevated error rates” / “increased latency”), but avoid exact internal thresholds
- Draft must include explicit uncertainty when appropriate (e.g., “We are investigating reports of…”).

C) Publishing to Statuspage
- Add Statuspage API client wrapper with:
  - retry/backoff for transient failures
  - idempotency keys to prevent duplicate posts
  - per-request audit log entry (internal storage)
- Support publishing:
  - incident update text
  - component status changes (limited to selected impacted_components)
  - incident lifecycle events (create incident on Statuspage if none exists; otherwise update)
- Guardrails:
  - require explicit human action to publish (no auto-publish)
  - on publish, post a confirmation + link in #incidents and tag Comms Lead

D) Roles + reminders
- Ensure incident-bot assigns Comms Lead role (or prompts IC to assign) for Sev0/Sev1.
- Remind Comms Lead if no public update is published within target window (TTFU target aligned with SRE standards).

E) Approval flow (Sev0/Sev1)
- Optional approval step before publish for Sev0/Sev1:
  - Approver: Support lead on duty OR IC (configurable)
  - Implement as Slack interactive approval with timeout + fallback to IC
- For Sev2+ publish should remain lightweight (no approval gate) unless explicitly toggled.

Privacy / security constraints (must-have)
- No customer names, IDs, or ticket references in drafts.
- No statements that over-promise SLA/credits.
- Avoid describing customer-specific capacity constraints or region-specific provider issues unless approved by IC.
- Security review required for Statuspage API secrets handling (token storage, rotation, least privilege).

Implementation notes
- Source of truth for components is the component registry (owned by SRE/Infra per ADR-014). incident-bot should read registry via the config package (don’t duplicate lists in code).
- Use structured incident state fields so we can later auto-generate publishable post-incident summaries (ties into ENG-4823).
- Audit log should record: incident_id, statuspage_incident_id, update_id, actor, timestamp, component_ids, phase, text hash.

Acceptance criteria
- [ ] During incident intake, IC/Comms Lead can select impacted components/regions/capabilities; validation prevents invalid IDs.
- [ ] For any incident phase, Comms Lead can generate a draft that matches the approved templates (minimal manual edits needed).
- [ ] Publishing succeeds with retry/idempotency; bot posts confirmation and stores audit record.
- [ ] Sev0/Sev1 approval path works end-to-end and does not block if approver unavailable (fallback policy documented).
- [ ] Secrets handling reviewed by Security/Compliance (Naomi Feldman) and approved for production.
- [ ] Runbook updates linked from bot UX (status-page update runbook).

Rollout plan
1) Ship behind feature flag: statuspage_publish_enabled=false by default.
2) Dogfood in staging Statuspage for game-day.
3) Enable for SRE on-call rotation only.
4) Expand to Infra on-call + incident commanders after 1–2 weeks if accuracy audit results are acceptable.

Links
- Project brief: Status page transparency upgrades (Confluence)
- Templates: Incident communication templates (Confluence)
- Runbook: Status page update runbook (Confluence)
- Related PRs (planned/active):
  - incident-bot PR: add component fields to incident intake
  - incident-bot PR: generate status update drafts from incident state
  - incident-bot PR: add Comms Lead role and reminders
  - incident-bot PR: Statuspage API client with retry and audit log
  - incident-bot PR: approval step for Sev0/Sev1

Open questions
- Do we allow publishing component status changes separately from incident update text, or always coupled?
- For “latency-only” degradations, do we standardize to “Degraded Performance” only (no Partial Outage) unless error rates also elevated?
- Who is the default approver for Sev0/Sev1 when Support lead is offline (IC vs Comms Lead)?

Background
We’re upgrading public status page transparency (component granularity + consistent comms + publishable post-incident summaries). Today incident-bot helps with incident intake and internal coordination, but status updates are still largely manual and inconsistent (TTFU misses, incorrect component selection, copy drift).

This ticket adds a Statuspage integration into incident-bot so we can:
1) capture component/region/capability impact fields as part of incident intake, and keep them current
2) generate status update drafts (Investigating/Identified/Mitigating/Monitoring/Resolved) from incident state + known facts
3) publish updates to Statuspage via API with audit logging + safe retry semantics
4) support an approval step for Sev0/Sev1 where required (Support lead or IC), without adding significant on-call burden

Goals
- Reduce time-to-first-update (TTFU) by making it easy to draft + publish a compliant first status update within target window.
- Improve correctness: ensure updates reference the right public components (product surface x region x capability) per the component registry.
- Standardize language: follow the incident communication templates and tone guidance.
- Create an internal audit log of what was published, by whom, and when (for later accuracy audits and post-incident summary generation).

Non-goals
- Auto-detect incidents or auto-change public component state directly from metrics (handled by observability signals/anti-flap work).
- Building a full Statuspage UI inside Slack.
- Publishing any customer-identifying details or capacity/internal architecture specifics.

Functional requirements
A) Incident intake fields (required)
- Add/require structured fields:
  - impacted_components: list of status component IDs (from registry)
  - impacted_regions: list (e.g., us-east, us-west, eu-west)
  - capabilities: chat | streaming | embeddings | reranking | console | dedicated-control-plane (as applicable)
  - impact_type: degraded_performance | partial_outage | major_outage
  - customer_impact_summary: short, safe statement (no customer names)
- Validation:
  - component IDs must exist in registry
  - region/capability must be compatible with selected components (best-effort validation; warn if mismatch)

2) Dogfood in staging Statuspage for game-day.
3) Enable for SRE on-call rotation only.

Links

title: Runtime: export queue depth + scheduler lag metrics for overload protection (low-cardinality)
preview:
We need a small set of runtime-exported capacity signals that can be used by routing/admission control to trigger tier-aware overload protection (gate best-effort first, preserve enterprise budgets). The signals must be stable, cheap to compute, and safe from high-cardinality blowups.

This ticket implements runtime instrumentation + export for:
- Queue depth (per model pool and optionally per route group)
- Scheduler lag (time-in-queue / dispatch delay)
- Optional supporting signals to validate correctness (active requests, batch size, kv cache pressure) while keeping tag set bounded

Constraints:
- Must not add meaningful overhead to the hot path (avoid per-request tagging work)
- Cardinality must be explicitly bounded (no tenant_id/customer_id, no raw route, no prompt length buckets at high resolution)
- Must support per-route aggregation via a small route_group / endpoint_group dimension derived upstream (gateway/routing) rather than raw path

Why: Without these signals, routing can’t reliably detect saturation early enough, leading to tail-latency cascades and 5xx on enterprise routes during bursts.

title: Game day plan: predictive autoscaling failure injection + success criteria
preview:
## Summary
Run a structured game day for Dedicated predictive autoscaling (with customer min/max bounds) prior to GA expansion. We will inject common failures (forecast outages/staleness, traffic ramps/spikes, stuck scale-down, capacity shortages) and verify:
- safety invariants (never exceed max; never drop below min; no scale-to-zero regressions)
- deterministic, auditable decisioning (requested desired, bounded desired, reasons)
- alerting + dashboards provide actionable signals
- on-call mitigations are safe and documented

## Background / context
Predictive mode biases the autoscaler using forecasted demand; reactive signals must still override incorrect predictions (while respecting bounds). During pilot we saw at least one unsafe edge case when predictor returned null (hotfix landed), and we need confidence we can safely operate under partial outages and noisy inputs.

Relevant artifacts:
- Confluence architecture: sources/confluence/eng-infra/gpu-fleet-and-capacity/autoscaler-bounds-and-predictive-signals-architecture.json
- ADR semantics/precedence: sources/confluence/architecture-and-standards/decision-records/adr-autoscaling-bounds-precedence-and-safety-invariants.json
- SLO/alerting updates: sources/confluence/eng-sre/slo-and-error-budgets/dedicated-autoscaling-slo-and-alerting-updates.json
- Runbook (draft): sources/confluence/eng-infra/runbooks/dedicated-autoscaler-troubleshooting-runbook.json
- Dashboard spec: sources/confluence/eng-platform/dashboards-and-alerts/autoscaling-bounds-observability-dashboard-spec.json

## Goals
1) Validate failure-mode behavior of predictive autoscaling under real-ish load.
2) Validate alerting (signal quality, noise) and on-call actions.
3) Validate auditability and postmortem readiness.
4) Produce a clear go/no-go recommendation for expanding pilot and GA.

## Non-goals
- Tune forecasting model quality beyond basic guardrails.
- Cross-region placement optimization.
- Contract or billing policy changes.

## Proposed date/time
Target: 2025-09-05 10:00–12:00 PT (2 hours)
Backup: 2025-09-06 09:00–11:00 PT

## Participants / roles
- Game day lead (facilitator + timeline): Rafael Mendes (SRE)
- Incident commander (if we create customer impact in pilot env): Miles Thornton (SRE)
- Autoscaler SME / operator: Vanessa Ortiz (Fleet)
- Predictive pipeline SME: Mei Lin (Data/pipeline)
- Control plane policy SME (schema/validation): Jordan Lee (Policy engine)
- Observability/dashboards: Sergio Costa (Observability)
- Smart routing observer (capacity signals consumer): Mina Haddad (Routing)
- Scribe (notes + action items): Leila Farouk (E2E test automation)

Escalation contacts (if needed):
- Neha Kapoor (Infra/reliability exec sponsor)
- Priya Natarajan (Platform exec sponsor)

## Environment / scope
- Use canary/pilot region only (us-west-2) with feature flag scoped to test account + one internal Dedicated deployment.
- Do NOT run on production customer deployments unless explicitly approved in advance.
- Traffic source: replay harness / canary generator from ENG-22147 (or perf-canary scenarios) at controlled QPS.

## Preconditions (must be true before starting)
- Feature flag per-account/deployment is working and reversible quickly (ENG-22148).
- Dashboards and alerts deployed to canary folder and visible to participants (ENG-22145).
- Audit events emitted and queryable for at least 24h (ENG-22146).
- Runbook has:
  - how to disable predictive mode per deployment
  - how to raise min temporarily (and how to revert)
  - how to identify bound-hit vs capacity shortage vs predictor staleness
- Confirm hotfix for null prediction is present in the running build (ref PR: sources/github/redwood/pr-48321-hotfix-prevent-scale-to-zero-when-predictor-returns-null.json).

## Comms plan
- Dedicated on-call channel: #eng-oncall
- Infra coordination: #eng-infra
- SRE coordination: #eng-sre
- If any unexpected user impact, switch to incident channel and stop injections.

## What we will monitor (during all scenarios)
- Queue depth, request latency p50/p95/p99, saturation signals
- Desired replicas vs bounded replicas
- Bound-hit counters (max-bound hit rate; min-bound enforcement)
- Forecast freshness (age), prediction vs actual demand error
- Scale event timeline (reasons + step size + cooldown state)
- Audit log events for every scaling decision and config change

## Failure injection scenarios
Each scenario includes: setup, injection, expected behavior, rollback/cleanup.

### Scenario A: Forecast outage (predictor unreachable)
Setup:
- Predictive mode enabled for test deployment.
Injection:
- Block forecast provider calls (simulate network policy drop) OR return 5xx from provider for 10 minutes.
Expected:
- Autoscaler falls back to reactive scaling within 1–2 evaluation intervals.
- No unsafe scale-down; min bound continues to be honored.
- A clear metric/log indicates fallback state and reason ("prediction_unavailable").
- Alert: Warning-level (not page) if outage < 15m; page only if sustained and associated with queue buildup (per SLO doc).
Rollback:
- Remove block / restore provider; confirm predictive resumes after freshness check passes.

### Scenario B: Stale forecast (forecast present but too old)
Setup:
- Predictive mode enabled; provider returns last-known forecast.
Injection:
- Freeze forecast updates; allow age to exceed staleness threshold.
Expected:
- Autoscaler ignores stale forecast and uses reactive.
- Observability shows forecast age and fallback reason ("prediction_stale").
- No oscillation when transitioning modes.
Rollback:
- Restore updates; verify resume.

### Scenario C: Bad forecast (systematic under-prediction during ramp)
Setup:
- Configure a known ramp (e.g., +30% QPS every 5 minutes for 30 minutes).
Injection:
- Provide forecast that is ~50% lower than actual.
Expected:
- Reactive saturation/queue depth overrides prediction (while respecting max bound).
- If max bound is hit, we should see:
  - bounded desired == max
  - sustained queue depth signal
  - max-bound saturation alert triggers at configured threshold
  - runbook path is clear (raise max, add capacity, or enable graceful degradation via routing)
Rollback:
- Restore accurate forecast; ramp down traffic.

### Scenario D: Bad forecast (systematic over-prediction causing overscale)
Setup:
- Start at steady state.
Injection:
- Provide forecast 2–3x actual for 30 minutes.
Expected:
- Scale-up should occur but must never exceed max bound.
- Cost/replica drift should be visible on dashboard.
- Scale-down should respect cooldown + dampening; no rapid oscillation.
- No alert paging unless churn/oscillation thresholds are crossed.
Rollback:
- Return forecast to normal; verify slow/controlled scale-down.

### Scenario E: Predictor returns null / empty payload
Setup:
- Predictive enabled.
Injection:
- Provider returns null/empty prediction for multiple intervals.
Expected:
- Autoscaler treats as prediction unavailable and falls back to reactive.
- Must not scale to zero.
- Must emit explicit metric/log ("prediction_null") and audit event reason.
Rollback:
- Restore normal provider behavior.

### Scenario F: Stuck scale-down protection (cooldown/dampening)
Setup:
- Create overscale state (e.g., by Scenario D) then reduce load quickly.
Injection:
- None beyond traffic drop; observe behavior.
Expected:
- Scale-down should happen gradually according to cooldown/dampening rules.
- Should not get stuck above max? (max is upper bound; being above max should not happen under invariants. If it does due to manual intervention, autoscaler should converge down safely.)
- If scale-down is prevented due to safety floors/min bound, this should be visible ("min_enforced").
Rollback:
- Return to baseline traffic; confirm stable replicas.

### Scenario G: Rapid config churn (policy updates)
Setup:
- Predictive enabled, normal load.
Injection:
- Attempt repeated policy updates (min/max toggles) within short window to validate rate limiting.
Expected:
- Control plane throttles/rate limits excessive changes; emits audit events and clear error messages.
- Autoscaler continues using last-known effective policy deterministically.
Rollback:
- Set final intended policy; wait for system to settle.

### Scenario H: Region capacity shortage (cannot satisfy min)
Setup:
- Set min to a value near current available capacity in canary fleet OR simulate by marking capacity unavailable.
Injection:
- Remove a portion of available GPU capacity.
Expected:
- System surfaces degraded state "min_unmet" with explicit metrics/alerts.
- Autoscaler does not flail; retry/backoff kicks in.
- On-call has clear mitigation steps (reduce min with customer approval, move to alternate pool/region if allowed, or activate routing degradation).
Rollback:
- Restore capacity; verify min becomes met and degraded state clears.

## Success criteria (go/no-go)
We consider the game day successful if:
1) Safety invariants: In all scenarios, replicas never exceed max, never drop below min, and no scale-to-zero occurs.
2) Determinism/auditability: For every scale decision/config change, we can retrieve:
   - requested desired
   - bounded desired
   - primary reason(s) (prediction_used, prediction_stale, reactive_override, max_enforced, min_enforced)
3) Observability: Dashboards clearly show bounded state, fallback mode, forecast freshness, and prediction error.
4) Alerts: We get pages only when expected (e.g., max-bound saturation + sustained queue buildup). No surprise paging for short predictor blips.
5) Mitigation: On-call can disable predictive and stabilize within 10 minutes using runbook-only steps.
6) Routing integration sanity: If bounded at max with sustained queue, routing sees bounded state and can apply configured graceful degradation (no feedback loop amplification).

If any of the following occur, we stop and file follow-ups before expanding pilot:
- Any violation of min/max bounds
- Scale-to-zero or near-zero behavior under prediction failures
- Unexplainable oscillation (e.g., repeated up/down steps within cooldown window)
- Alerts too noisy to be actionable

## Execution checklist
Before:
- Confirm feature flags and rollback procedure
- Confirm dashboards, alert routes, and on-call paging test
- Confirm traffic generator configs and stop button
- Confirm scribe doc link + action item template

During:
- Announce scenario start/end in #eng-sre
- Capture timestamps for injections and observed effects
- Record screenshots/links to graphs for each scenario

After:
- Summarize outcomes + gaps
- File issues for any unexpected behavior
- Update runbook with anything learned
- Provide go/no-go recommendation to PM (Lauren) and exec sponsors

## Output / deliverables
- Game day notes (timestamped) and action items list
- A brief write-up: what passed/failed, proposed fixes, updated gating criteria
- Updated runbook sections for predictor outage/staleness, bound saturation, min unmet

## Open questions
- Final paging thresholds for predictor outage vs queue buildup (align with ENG-22145)
- Who owns toggling predictive mode in Console during incident (SRE vs Fleet) for pilot accounts
- Whether to include a “routing degradation forced” step in the default mitigation flow

## Related
- ENG-22147: Load test and simulation replay for predictive mode
- ENG-22145: Alerts for max-bound saturation and sustained queue buildup
- ENG-22151: Region capacity shortage when min cannot be met
- Support ticket learnings: SUP-28479 (oscillation), SUP-28452 (min unmet)
